19.name: Joe Flacco
19.headline: Insurance Only: Flacco's Value Lives And Dies With Burrow
19.body:
Flacco's 2025 line tells the story of a stopgap, not a fantasy asset: 13 games, a QB24 finish on 146.7 points, 15 touchdowns against 10 picks, and a 60.3% completion rate. The underlying marks were uglier still, a minus-0.1 EPA per play, a 41.1% success rate, and a minus-2.8 CPOE that flagged accuracy slipping below replacement level. At 416 attempts he leaned heavily on a short, dink-and-dunk profile, with completed air yards of just 5.7 per catch and only a 10.8% deep-attempt rate.

Now entering his age-41 season back in Cincinnati, he sits firmly behind Joe Burrow with no standalone path to snaps. Leave him undrafted; an ADP near 299 confirms the market agrees. The lone case is contingency: when Flacco started six games here he was on a roughly 4,600-yard, 37-touchdown pace, so handcuff him only if Burrow's availability turns shaky.

3198.name: Derrick Henry
3198.headline: Still A Workhorse, But The Tape Is Slipping
3198.body:
Henry banked 264.5 fantasy points and an RB7 finish in 2025 on 307 carries, 1,595 yards, a 5.2 average and 16 rushing scores across all 17 games. Volume and the goal line stay locked in: 65 red-zone opportunities and 27 goal-line carries kept the touchdown floor sky-high even at age 31. That's the bankable part of the profile heading into his age-32 campaign.

The efficiency trend, though, demands attention. EPA per play collapsed from 0.143 to 0.019 year over year, yards after contact dipped to 2.25, and broken tackles cratered from 42 to just 12. Explosive-run rate slid to 11.7 percent as the bursts that defined 2024 thinned out. With Baltimore's interior line in flux, draft him as a touchdown-dependent RB1 in the back half of round one, not the dominant force he was a season ago.

3202.name: Austin Hooper
3202.headline: Hooper Returns to Atlanta as a Touch-Limited Afterthought
3202.body:
Hooper's 2025 line tells the story of a fading complementary piece: 13 games, a 5.4% target share on just 26 looks, and 263 yards with two scores good for TE45. He ran routes on only 37.6% of dropbacks and posted a 0.126 target-per-route-run rate, both well off his 2024 marks of 47% and 0.217. The efficiency flashes are real but empty calories for fantasy, a 0.646 EPA per play, a 73.1% success rate, and an 80.8% catch rate on tiny volume.

Now back in Atlanta, he slides in behind Kyle Pitts as the clear second tight end on a one-year deal. His ranking sits around TE59 with an ADP outside pick 440, and the math backs the gloom: five red-zone targets and a 1.27 yards-per-route-run figure won't move the needle. Until injury opens a path to snaps, treat him as a deep-league name to monitor rather than draft.

3257.name: Jacoby Brissett
3257.headline: Brissett's 2025 Spike Faces A Steep Comedown
3257.body:
Pressed into duty after Kyler Murray's injury, Brissett delivered the QB14 finish on 227.4 fantasy points across 14 games, throwing 23 touchdowns against just 8 picks. Dig under the volume, though, and the efficiency screams regression: 0.004 EPA per play, a 45.8% success rate, and only a 1.6 CPOE. He took 43 sacks behind a 27.5% pressure rate and posted a 17.6% bad-throw mark, so the production leaned heavily on opportunity rather than elite play.

Arizona hands him the keys again entering 2026, but the runway is short with Carson Beck and an unresolved contract hovering. A loaded supporting cast in McBride and Harrison Jr. props up the ceiling, yet the modest 8.7% deep-attempt rate and pedestrian air-yards profile cap upside. He's a viable QB2 and superflex target around the QB30 range, not a single-league starter worth chasing on last year's spike.

3294.name: Dak Prescott
3294.headline: Dak Prescott: bounce-back QB1 with top-five ceiling
3294.body:
Prescott reclaimed elite status in 2025, ranking third among quarterbacks with 313.8 fantasy points across a full 17 games after the 2024 ankle injury limited him to eight starts and a dismal QB31 finish. His 4,552 yards, 30 touchdowns and a 0.138 EPA per play confirmed the dip was health-driven, not decline, though his 47.4% success rate still trailed the 50% he posted at his 2023 peak. At 32, the efficiency floor is firmly established, and a 67.3% completion rate keeps the volume productive.

The surrounding talent only strengthens the case: a CeeDee Lamb, George Pickens and Jake Ferguson trio gives him a complete passing menu, and sharper red-zone conversion could push him toward the top five. He profiles as a draft-day bargain, with consensus rankings near QB11 and a price hovering around the eighth quarterback off the board. Modest passing-volume regression is plausible, but the discount makes him a near-lock value at QB1.

3634.name: Kalif Raymond
3634.headline: Efficient depth piece who needs a route to volume
3634.body:
Raymond's 2025 line tells the story of a high-floor role player rather than a fantasy asset. On just 36.6% of snaps he ran 214 routes, drew 30 targets and a 5.5% target share, and turned them into 24 grabs for 289 yards and a score. The underlying marks stay sharp: a 0.507 EPA per play, a 66.7% success rate, an 80% catch rate, and zero drops, with 1.35 yards per route run. That efficiency is real, but it never scales because the volume isn't there.

Now 31 and on a fresh deal in Chicago, he profiles as a depth receiver buried behind the Bears' top options, with a draft cost deep past WR100 reflecting that. His value remains rooted in special teams and spot snaps. Unless an injury opens meaningful route share, treat him as a name to monitor rather than draft in any standard format.

4017.name: Deshaun Watson
4017.headline: The numbers say bench him until proven otherwise
4017.body:
The efficiency profile is alarming. Across seven 2024 games Watson posted a brutal minus-0.23 EPA per play and a 34.3% success rate, finishing as QB39 with just five touchdowns against three picks. His 78.7% on-target mark and minus-2.2 CPOE show the accuracy slipped, and a 31.9% pressure rate with 33 sacks meant he never operated cleanly. The 2023 sample was only marginally better: minus-0.09 EPA and a 41.6% success rate over six starts.

The rushing equity that once propped up his ceiling has evaporated, leaving 148 yards and one score on 31 carries in 2024. He enters camp in an open competition for the starting job, drafted around QB32 in single-QB formats where he carries no standalone value. An upgraded receiving corps offers a faint path back, but until the efficiency stabilizes, he is a Superflex dart at best and nothing more.

4034.name: Christian McCaffrey
4034.headline: Bounce-back RB2 finish proves the engine still runs
4034.body:
Last year erased any doubt the 2024 layoff created: McCaffrey posted 314.6 fantasy points and graded out as our RB2 over 17 games, pairing a 23.5% target share with 102 catches for 924 yards. The receiving floor is the separator. He turned 531 routes into seven scores and stacked 100 red-zone opportunities with 20 goal-line carries, the kind of every-down, every-zone deployment that almost no back commands. An 0.041 EPA per play with 83.1% snaps confirms the volume was earned, not gifted.

The caution is age and tread, not talent. At 29 turning 30, his 3.86 yards per carry and 1.52 yards after contact have softened from the 5.36 and 2.01 he ran in 2023, and only 8.68% of carries went explosive. San Francisco is already managing his workload through camp. Draft him as a back-end first-rounder, lean on the pass-game usage as insurance, and stash Jordan James as the handcuff.

4046.name: Patrick Mahomes
4046.headline: Mahomes returns from ACL with legs and value in question
4046.body:
Before the Week 15 knee injury, Mahomes was building his most efficient season in years: a career-best 0.16 EPA per play across 14 games, a 49% success rate, and 284.7 fantasy points despite a modest 22 passing touchdowns against 11 picks. The real engine was his rushing, where he posted career highs of 422 yards and five scores on 64 carries. That mobility, not the 62.7% completion rate or middling 7.15 yards per attempt, carried his QB12 finish and separated him from a passing line that has quietly cooled three straight years.

The problem for 2026 is that the legs are exactly what a torn ACL and LCL threaten. He is targeting Week 1 but still limited to 7-on-7 work in June, and Kansas City answered by importing Kenneth Walker III to lighten his load. With no new receiving help around Kelce and a healthy Rice, expect a more pocket-bound passer. He profiles as a low-end QB1 worth a mid-round flier, not an every-week anchor.

4066.name: Evan Engram
4066.headline: Engram's Denver Debut Cratered, And 2026 Offers Little Relief
4066.body:
Engram's first Denver season ranked as a TE25 disappointment: 50 grabs, 461 yards and a single touchdown across 16 games, good for just 52.8 fantasy points. The underlying profile was worse than the box score. He ran routes on only 56.9% of dropbacks, drew a 13% target share, and posted a 0.247 WOPR while drawing only 4.36 air yards per target on shallow, low-leverage routes. A 39.5% success rate and 0.058 EPA per play barely registered, and eight drops on 58 catchable balls didn't help.

That collapse traces straight back to his elite 2023 (TE3, 0.454 WOPR, 53.8% success rate), making the regression undeniable. Denver's pass-catching room is now overloaded after adding Waddle alongside Sutton, Mims and Franklin, plus rookie tight ends behind him. At a TE35 ADP, he's a touchdown-dependent dart throw, not a starter worth rostering before October.

4137.name: James Conner
4137.headline: A torn 2025 leaves Conner fighting for relevance
4137.body:
Conner's 2025 evaporated after three games, and the tape was ugly: 2.97 yards per carry, a -0.013 EPA per play, and just a 3.12 percent explosive-run rate as the foot injury sapped his burst. That cratered him to RB87 with only 25.3 fantasy points. The contrast with his prior form is stark. In 2024 he finished RB9 across 16 games on 1,094 rushing yards, 4.64 per carry, eight scores, and a 14.41 percent explosive-run rate, while 2023 paired 5.0 per carry with a strong 0.075 EPA per play.

The efficiency is real when he's upright, but the path narrowed. Arizona spent a premium pick on a back and added depth, threatening to push the 31-year-old into a committee or worse. Treat him as a late-round dart with clear standalone upside only if the rookie stumbles or a trade reopens the lead role he commanded for four years.

4147.name: Samaje Perine
4147.headline: A Steady Cincinnati Backup Drafted Only as Insurance
4147.body:
Perine's 2025 line tells the story of a complementary back doing exactly what's asked of him: 84 carries for 382 yards at a tidy 4.55 per rush, plus 17 grabs on 21 targets across 15 games for 60.9 fantasy points. The efficiency held up, but the volume didn't, and a 35.1% snap share with a 3.5% target rate keeps the ceiling low. A minus-0.027 EPA per play and just 1.99 yards after contact per attempt confirm he's a rotational piece, not a feature runner, even with three rushing scores and two 20-plus chunk runs mixed in.

The role behind Chase Brown caps his standalone value, since most of Perine's appeal lives in third-down work and the chance Brown misses time. He's a late-round handcuff and waiver-wire stash rather than a draft-day priority, with an RB72 market price. Grab him only if you roster Brown or play in deep formats.

4454.name: Kendrick Bourne
4454.headline: Efficient on a Shoestring, Buried in a Crowded Room
4454.body:
Bourne's 2025 tape was quietly excellent on volume nobody handed him. Across 14 games he turned just 53 targets into 37 grabs for 551 yards at 14.9 per catch, and the efficiency markers pop: a 0.388 EPA per play, a 64.2% success rate, and 1.62 yards per route run. The catch is the workload. A 9.6% target share, 0.155 targets per route, and a 0.24 WOPR are backup-receiver inputs, and the zero touchdowns capped a WR52 finish despite the strong per-rep production.

Arizona doesn't fix the volume problem. Bourne slots third or fourth behind Marvin Harrison Jr., Michael Wilson, and target magnet Trey McBride, projecting as a slot complement rather than a featured piece. The efficiency is real and the hands are dependable, but until injuries clear a path, this is a deep-bench dart and a bye-week patch, not a weekly starter.

4866.name: Saquon Barkley
4866.headline: Aging Barkley a steady RB1 with diminished ceiling
4866.body:
Our model ranked Barkley the RB11 in 2025 at 195.3 fantasy points across 16 games, a clear step back from his RB1, 322.3-point monster of 2024. The volume stayed elite, but the explosion vanished: yards per carry fell sharply, his rushing scores were nearly halved, and his breakaway-run rate dropped. His EPA per play slipped negative, signaling the 2024 outlier season has corrected toward a more ordinary profile.

Now entering his age-29 season, he profiles as a high-floor RB1 rather than a league-winner. The workhorse role is secure, but an aging line and a career carry count climbing toward 2,000 invite caution. Expert boards back that read, placing him around RB7-8 with a top-15 floor; weigh him against younger backs before reaching early.

4881.name: Lamar Jackson
4881.headline: Lamar Jackson eyes elite QB1 rebound after injuries
4881.body:
Our advanced metrics tell two stories. In 2024 Jackson ranked sixth among quarterbacks with a monstrous 430.4 fantasy points across 17 games, posting a 0.284 EPA per play, 51.3% success rate, 41 passing touchdowns to four picks, and 915 rushing yards. Last season was a different animal: limited to 13 games, he slid to QB23 with just 214.9 points, 21 touchdowns, and his efficiency collapsed to a 0.067 EPA per play and 45.9% success rate, while rushing output cratered.

The gap is almost entirely health and a shrinking ground role rather than lost talent. A cascade of injuries gutted his trademark mobility and clouded his usage down the stretch. Still, drafters are treating the down year as noise, slotting him as a top-two quarterback in the early rounds. If his legs return, the dual-threat ceiling that produced an elite 2024 makes him a league-winning value.

4983.name: DJ Moore
4983.headline: Allen's new toy buys back Moore's WR1 ceiling
4983.body:
Moore's 2025 was a study in collapse: a WR39 finish on just 682 yards, a 16% target share, and a career-low 1.30 yards per route as Chicago funneled work to Rome Odunze and Colston Loveland. His 0.162 targets per route and 45.9% success rate were both shadows of the 0.252 and 57.4% he posted in 2023, when he ranked sixth at the position with 1,364 yards and a sterling 0.487 EPA per play.

Buffalo flips the variable that mattered most. Pairing with Josh Allen reopens the explosive ceiling Moore showed at peak, even if a run-leaning attack and a crowded room cap his volume near the double-digit-target range he saw last year. The red-zone usage is thin and the floor is volatile, but the talent and quarterback upgrade make him a defensible Round 5 swing on rebound equity.

4984.name: Josh Allen
4984.headline: Josh Allen stays the QB1 floor with rushing ceiling
4984.body:
Our model put Allen 11th among quarterbacks in 2025, a slip in positional rank that undersells the season: he set a personal best with a 69.3% completion rate, threw for 3,668 yards and 25 scores against just 10 picks, and added 579 yards and 14 touchdowns on the ground. That dual-threat profile delivered 364.6 fantasy points, the third straight year north of 360 after 392.6 in 2023 and 379 in 2024. His 0.171 EPA per play dipped from 2024's elite 0.311, and a 48.9% success rate signals modest passing-efficiency regression worth monitoring.

The rushing equity is the floor that makes him matchup-proof, with 500-plus yards and a dozen-plus scores on the ground in each of the last three seasons. A deeper receiver room should lift his aerial volume after years of thin perimeter help. Treat him as a back-half second-round pick in single-quarterback formats and the unquestioned first selection in superflex builds.

4993.name: Mike Gesicki
4993.headline: Gesicki's 2025 collapse leaves no fantasy floor
4993.body:
The 2024 version of Gesicki ranked 10th at the position on a 0.292 WOPR, 13.2% target share, and 83 targets that turned into 665 yards. None of that carried over. In 2025 he slid to TE36, drawing just 42 targets across 12 games for a 6.9% target share and a 0.175 target-per-route-run rate, while his route share cratered to 35.7% on a 38.5% snap rate. The 307 yards and two scores read like a backup line because that is what the role became.

The efficiency markers still flicker, a 0.127 EPA per play and 57.1% success rate, but volume that thin caps the ceiling. Cincinnati funnels everything to Chase, Higgins, and Chase Brown, with Fant and Sample splitting the leftovers behind him. He profiles as a touchdown-dependent dart in TE-premium and deep formats only, and a waiver name everywhere else until the snaps return.

5008.name: Durham Smythe
5008.headline: A Blocking Specialist Without a Fantasy Pulse
5008.body:
Smythe's 2025 line tells the whole story: 7 games, a 25.4% snap share, and just 6 targets on 101 routes for a 1.1% target share and 0.059 targets per route run. He turned that into 4 catches, 25 yards, no scores, and a microscopic 2.5 fantasy points, while his -0.042 EPA per play and 33.3% success rate confirmed there was no efficiency hiding underneath the volume. That is a steep slide from the 28th-ranked TE finish he posted back in 2023.

Now in Baltimore, he profiles purely as an in-line blocker behind a featured starter, which only narrows an already empty passing-game footprint. With a TE78 valuation and effectively zero roster relevance, there is no realistic path to weekly points. He stays off fantasy radars in every format and should be left on the wire outside of deep two-TE dynasty rosters needing snaps.

5012.name: Mark Andrews
5012.headline: Andrews' Efficiency Cratered, But The Volume Lever Is There
5012.body:
The 2025 tape was a problem. Andrews dropped to TE27 on 1.27 yards per route run, a fall from his TE9 mark a year earlier when he posted 1.96. His EPA per play collapsed from 0.726 to a flat 0.022, and his 50% success rate told the same story as the 8.79 yards per reception. He still drew 70 targets and a 17.2% share across all 17 games, but the production thinned to 48 grabs, 422 yards, and five scores.

The path back is real. He held 15 red-zone looks and nine end-zone targets, so the touchdown equity that fueled his 11-score 2024 hasn't vanished. With Isaiah Likely gone to New York, Andrews is the only proven seam threat in Baltimore, and a healthier Lamar Jackson lifts the floor. He's a late-round dart with mid-range TE1 ceiling if the offense normalizes.

5045.name: Courtland Sutton
5045.headline: Sutton's Touchdowns Hide Thinning Volume in Crowded Denver
5045.body:
Sutton's WR12 finish in 2025 was built on opportunity more than dominance: a 21.2% target share, 124 targets across 17 games, and a 34.4% air-yards share that kept him the engine of Denver's downfield game. The efficiency, though, slipped from the prior year, with EPA per play climbing to 0.359 but yards per route run dipping to 1.74 and a 0.212 target-per-route rate that trails his 2024 mark. Seven scores on 74 grabs and 19 red-zone looks carried the fantasy line.

That touchdown dependence is the worry now. Bo Nix has leaned on him two straight seasons, but the arrival of Jaylen Waddle splinters a passing tree with little fresh volume to absorb, nudging Sutton toward a 1B role. His 1,017 yards and contested deep work keep a floor intact, yet the ceiling narrows. Treat him as a WR3 worth grabbing around the mid-rounds rather than reaching early.

5086.name: Marquez Valdes-Scantling
5086.headline: A deep-shot specialist running out of runway
5086.body:
The arrow points down for Valdes-Scantling, and the numbers explain why. His 2025 stint in Pittsburgh cratered to a 5.3% target share across nine games, with a minus-0.105 EPA per play, a 41.4% success rate, and just 0.53 yards per route run on 226 routes. He caught 48.3% of his looks for 120 yards and a single score, landing 139th at the position. That is a far cry from his 2024 ceiling in New Orleans, where 411 yards and four touchdowns rode a 1.51 YPRR and a 17.9% air-yards share.

Now 31 and in Dallas, his fantasy case lives entirely on volume he hasn't earned in a year. He remains a true vertical threat, but route counts and target rate have eroded each season. Unless the depth chart hands him meaningful snaps, treat him as a late-round dart at best and a waiver-wire afterthought in most formats.

5127.name: Kyle Allen
5127.headline: Allen joins Buffalo's QB room strictly as insurance
5127.body:
The advanced profile underscores how marginal Allen's recent footprint has been. He logged two games in 2025 and went 0-for-2 with a -0.271 EPA per play and a -63.7 CPOE, posting -0.3 fantasy points on the season. The 2024 sample was equally tiny: one attempt, one completion for 19 yards. There simply isn't enough volume to project anything meaningful, and his career arc points squarely toward a clipboard role.

Now in Buffalo on a modest two-year deal, Allen sits buried on the depth chart behind an entrenched starter. Expert consensus pegs him outside the top 70 quarterbacks with no ADP and roughly zero rostership, and that ranking is generous given his path to snaps. Treat him as pure emergency insurance: unless an injury thrusts him into starts, there is no scenario where he belongs on a fantasy roster in 2026.

5906.name: Dawson Knox
5906.headline: Knox is touchdown-dependent insurance in a crowded Buffalo room
5906.body:
Knox quietly turned in his most efficient season in 2025, finishing as TE28 with 36 grabs on 49 looks for 417 yards and four scores across 16 games. The underlying work backs it up: a 0.335 EPA per play, a 63.3% success rate, and 1.45 yards per route run, all firmly up from his 2024 line. He cleared 12 red-zone targets and seven end-zone looks, which is where his fantasy juice still lives, and his 73.5% catch rate with only two drops showed reliable hands.

The ceiling stays capped because the numbers come on a thin diet. A 10.2% target share and 53.9% route rate confirm a true time-share with Dalton Kincaid, leaving Knox as a touchdown-or-bust streamer. Drafted near the back of the position, he is best treated as a Kincaid handcuff who only earns standalone weeks if injury opens the room. Hold him for the scores, not the volume.

5917.name: Mecole Hardman
5917.headline: Buried in Buffalo with no path to snaps
5917.body:
The trend line is brutal. After landing in Buffalo, Hardman logged just two games in 2025 on a 3.5% snap share, ran two routes, drew one target and caught nothing, posting a ghastly minus-0.508 EPA per play. That followed a 2024 in Kansas City where he salvaged 12 grabs for 90 yards across 12 games (1.32 yards per route run, 0.308 EPA) but a 2.4% target share and zero touchdowns. His last semblance of a role came in 2023: 24 targets, 15 catches, 124 yards over seven games.

He re-upped with the Bills off the practice squad, which tells you all you need to know about his standing. There is no projectable target volume here and no clear lane on a crowded depth chart. Ranked outside the top 500 and rostered in essentially no leagues, Hardman is a deep dynasty afterthought until he forces meaningful snaps that have not arrived.

5938.name: Lil'Jordan Humphrey
5938.headline: Deep-roster Bronco with a usage ceiling he can't crack
5938.body:
The profile hasn't budged. Across seven games in 2025, Humphrey logged just 25 targets and a 5% target share, turning them into 13 catches for 156 yards and a score on a thin 0.95 yards per route run. His 0.317 EPA per play and 48% success rate read fine in isolation, but at a 26% route rate they're a sample, not a role. Even his 2024 high-water mark topped out at an 8.2% target share, so the ranking sitting at 130 among wideouts reflects the opportunity, not bad luck.

Denver brought him back for a familiar depth job, and the room ahead of him is crowded enough that he projects as a special-teams body more than a target earner. He's off the draft board in every format, with an ADP near pick 570 and essentially zero rostership. File him as an injury-contingency name only.

5995.name: Justice Hill
5995.headline: Pass-down change-up with a Henry-sized upside ceiling
5995.body:
Hill's profile reads as a satellite back, not a workhorse. Across 10 games in 2025 he logged just 18 carries against 27 targets, hauling in 21 catches for 169 yards and a 6.6% target share, while his snap rate slid to 37.1%. The efficiency stayed real — 5.17 yards per carry, 3.89 yards after contact per attempt, and a 71-yard burst that flashed the breakaway speed Baltimore values — but a minus-0.019 EPA per play and only 44.2 fantasy points underline how thin the volume runs.

The 2024 tape is the tell: 51 targets, 42 grabs, three receiving scores and a plus-0.151 EPA per play when he actually got fed. With the primary backup role to Derrick Henry now his alone, Hill stays a deep-league passing-down piece on standalone value. The genuine appeal is contingency — if Henry misses time, this skill set converts into instant RB2 production overnight.

6001.name: Drew Sample
6001.headline: Sample's blocking floor isn't a fantasy ceiling
6001.body:
Our numbers paint Sample as exactly what he is: a complementary piece whose receiving usage barely registers. He ran 286 routes across 12 games in 2025 yet drew only 20 targets, a 3.3% share that translated to 0.37 yards per route run and a 68th-ranked finish at the position. Fifteen grabs, 106 yards and a single score netted just 16.9 fantasy points. The efficiency markers stay quietly respectable, a 0.133 EPA per play and 40% success rate with zero drops, but volume this thin caps any standalone value.

The trend isn't encouraging either. His 2024 line carried sharper marks, 0.217 EPA and a 59.1% success rate, and his 28.3-point 2023 still towers over recent output. With Cincinnati's targets funneling elsewhere, Sample profiles as a blocking-first tight end and deep-bench afterthought, draftable only if injuries hand him a genuine route-tree role.

6011.name: Gardner Minshew
6011.headline: A backup arm chasing one more cameo as a starter
6011.body:
The trend line is unkind. Minshew's last meaningful stretch came in 2023, when he started all 16 for Indianapolis and finished QB17 on 3,305 yards and a 15-to-9 touchdown-to-pick split. Since then it has cratered: a 2024 split in Las Vegas produced a 9-to-10 ratio, a 43.8% success rate and minus-0.154 EPA per play across 10 games, then 2025 brought just 13 attempts of replacement-level noise, a minus-0.368 EPA mark and a brutal minus-20.5 CPOE in four appearances.

Now he lands in Arizona as insurance behind Jacoby Brissett, with a rookie also angling for the No. 2 job. Projections peg him near 9.7 points per game and a QB46-ish draft slot, which is to say undraftable in standard formats. Stash him only in deep superflex builds, and only if Brissett falters and the snaps actually open up.

6039.name: Ty Johnson
6039.headline: Efficient Bills passing-down back with little volume to mine
6039.body:
Johnson's profile is built on efficiency, not volume. Across all 17 games in 2025 he posted a positive 0.171 EPA per play on just a 29.6% snap share, turning 50 carries into 200 yards (4.0 per pop) and three scores while chipping in 24 catches on 33 targets for 263 yards and two more touchdowns. A 6.9% target share and 1.16 yards per route run confirm he's a complementary piece, and the 76.3 fantasy points reflect a five-touchdown ceiling that requires red-zone luck to repeat.

The underlying quality is real: his 0.365 EPA per play and 5.2 yards per carry in 2024 rank among the league's best for his usage tier. But that efficiency rarely converts to startable weeks behind Buffalo's depth chart. As a passing-downs specialist projected for roughly 71 points and an ADP outside the top 250, he's a deep-bench handcuff and bye-week dart, not a draft-day priority.

6136.name: Jarrett Stidham
6136.headline: A capable insurance policy waiting behind Bo Nix
6136.body:
The last meaningful sample tells the story of a steady backup, not a breakout. Across his most recent two-game look, Stidham completed 60.6% of his 66 throws for 496 yards, two scores and one pick, posting essentially neutral production at -0.001 EPA per play and a 41.5% success rate. His -3.7 CPOE and a 19.4% bad-throw rate confirm he hovers below the league line as a passer, with a 23.7% pressure rate and 7 sacks showing the offense around him wobbled in those reps.

The outlook is purely contingency. He sits behind Bo Nix in Denver and only surfaces if Nix misses time, exactly the path that handed him an AFC title-game start. Carrying no real draft capital and a QB ranking outside the top 55, he is droppable in standard formats and a dart-throw stash only in deep Superflex and two-quarterback builds banking on an injury upstream.

6271.name: Olamide Zaccheaus
6271.headline: Falcons reunion can't outrun a sliding profile
6271.body:
Zaccheaus took a clear step back in 2025, finishing WR89 on 65 targets and a 12.2% share across 14 games in Chicago. His efficiency cratered: a minus-0.044 EPA per play, a 44.6% success rate, and just 1.0 yards per route run after posting 1.9 the year prior. The 8.0-yard average per catch and 313 receiving yards tell the story of a low-leverage role, even with 12 red-zone looks producing only two scores. The juice he flashed in 2024 (0.392 EPA, 56.2% success) simply didn't carry over.

Now back in Atlanta, he profiles as depth behind Drake London with rookie competition pushing for the same slot snaps. Mooney's exit opens a sliver of volume, but a 0.207 target-per-route rate and shrinking air-yards share cap the ceiling. He's a late-round dart and waiver watch, nothing more, until the route share actually expands.

6290.name: Scotty Miller
6290.headline: Deep-threat dart who needs a real role to matter
6290.body:
Miller arrives in Chicago as a vertical specialist whose recent profile screams fifth option. He logged just five games in 2025 with a 17.3% route share, a 2.7% target share, and a 0.14 targets-per-route-run mark, finishing WR169 on 6.2 fantasy points. The underlying efficiency cratered too: a minus-0.079 EPA per play and 42.9% success rate, with zero red-zone scores on 14 targets. There is no ambiguity here about where he sits on the depth chart.

The ceiling case rests on the field-stretching flashes. Back in 2023 he turned 16 targets into 161 yards and two touchdowns at 1.35 yards per route run and a 0.537 EPA per play, with a 56-yard long. Those splash plays are real, but his career WOPR has never cleared 0.08. Until snaps climb, Miller is a best-ball dart throw and nothing more in 2026.

6451.name: Stephen Carlson
6451.headline: Carlson is roster depth, not a fantasy bet
6451.body:
At 29 and listed at 6-4, 248, the Princeton product sits buried on Chicago's tight end depth chart, stuck behind Colston Loveland and Cole Kmet. Expert consensus pegs him near TE116 with no meaningful ADP, and he's rostered in essentially zero leagues. His path to snaps runs entirely through injuries ahead of him; absent that, the blocking-tilted role caps any receiving upside.

The schedule offers no rescue either, with Chicago's tight ends carrying one of the league's tougher slates. Until the depth chart thins out, Carlson is a deep-bench stash at best, the kind of name you monitor only if Kmet or Loveland misses time. Leave him on waivers in standard formats and revisit only when an opportunity actually opens. There's nothing here worth a draft pick in 2026.

6768.name: Tua Tagovailoa
6768.headline: Tua's Falcons Reset Is A Stay-Away Until He Wins The Job
6768.body:
The arrow is pointing the wrong way. After ranking as the QB1 in 2023 (4,624 yards, 29 scores, 270.4 fantasy points), Tagovailoa cratered to QB22 last year, throwing for just 2,660 yards with a 20-to-15 touchdown-to-interception split across 14 games. His efficiency collapsed too: EPA per play fell from 0.195 in 2024 to a near-flat 0.004, and his success rate slid to 43.8%. That is not a quarterback you draft on reputation.

Now in Atlanta on a one-year deal, he steps into an open competition with Michael Penix Jr. and is no lock to start, even with Penix rehabbing a knee. The schedule is soft and Drake London plus Kyle Pitts give him real weapons, but the QB31-range price tag tells the story. Leave him on waivers in single-quarterback formats and treat him as a late dart only in superflex.

6770.name: Joe Burrow
6770.headline: Burrow's Elite Efficiency Is Bottled, Just Add Health
6770.body:
The 2024 tape is the real Burrow: a wire-to-wire QB1 across all 17 games, piling up 372.8 fantasy points on 43 touchdowns against nine picks while posting a 0.159 EPA per play, a 51.1% success rate and a 6.8 CPOE that screamed best-quarterback-alive. A toe injury capped 2025 at eight games, yet even compromised he completed 66.8% of his throws with 17 scores to five interceptions, a 0.114 EPA per play and a still-excellent 4.7 CPOE. The accuracy never wavered.

That efficiency baseline is why the cratered counting stats lie. A full slate alongside Ja'Marr Chase and Tee Higgins in a fast-tempo, pass-leaning attack restores a top-three QB ceiling, and he profiles as a top-five pick at the position. The toe limited his scrambling to 14 carries; a return to mobility only widens the gap. Healthy, he is a value the moment passers come off the board.

6783.name: Jerry Jeudy
6783.headline: Jeudy's Efficiency Cratered, and Cleveland Reloaded
6783.body:
The volume held but everything around it collapsed. Jeudy still ran 91.6% of routes and commanded a 20.3% target share across 17 games, yet his per-play EPA fell off a cliff to -0.305 with a 35.8% success rate, both among the worst marks at the position. He turned 106 targets into just 50 catches for 602 yards and 2 scores, a 47.2% catch rate dragged down by 10 drops and a YPRR that sank from 1.81 to 1.08. The WR6 finish of 2024 felt like a different player entirely.

The role isn't safe, either. Cleveland spent early draft capital on two more wideouts, crowding a depth chart that no longer treats Jeudy as the centerpiece. A tweaked hamstring already has him on a cautious camp ramp. With a late-round price tag, he's a deep-league dart throw who needs early production to justify a roster spot.

6786.name: CeeDee Lamb
6786.headline: Lamb stays a WR1 even with shrunken volume
6786.body:
Lamb's 2025 was a clear step down from his peak: limited to 13 games, he posted 117 targets, 75 catches, 1,077 yards and just 3 touchdowns, finishing as our WR10 with 125.9 fantasy points. That's a steep fall from the WR2 monster of 2023 (181 targets, 1,749 yards, 12 scores, 268.2 points) and even 2024's WR8 line. His target share dipped to 19.3%, yet a 0.248 EPA per play and 54.7% success rate show the efficiency held when he was on the field.

The context behind the dip matters more than the raw decline. George Pickens' arrival siphoned looks, and a stingier Dallas defense trimmed the offense's overall snap count, capping the ceiling Lamb once owned as the lone alpha. Still, with Prescott driving the passing game and a top-10 draft cost, the talent and floor remain elite. Treat him as a rock-solid WR1 whose blow-up upside is now shared, not gone.

6790.name: D'Andre Swift
6790.headline: Swift's efficiency turnaround keeps him a sturdy RB2
6790.body:
Swift answered a rough 2024 with the best efficiency season of his career, pushing his yards per carry to 4.87 across 223 totes for 1,087 yards and nine scores, plus 34 grabs on a 9% target share. The jump shows up in the underlying numbers: his EPA per play flipped from minus-0.122 to a positive 0.051, his explosive-run rate nearly doubled to 13.45%, and he finished as our RB13 with 194.6 fantasy points. He also commanded the goal-line work, logging 14 inside carries and 41 red-zone opportunities.

The one drag is the backfield split. Swift owns the passing downs and two-minute snaps, but Chicago leaned harder on Kyle Monangai down the stretch, and the committee threatens to settle into a 1A/1B arrangement. Still, drafting near RB22 inside Ben Johnson's projected top-10 offense buys a high-floor back with weekly upside whenever the volume tilts his way.

6801.name: Tee Higgins
6801.headline: Elite efficiency, touchdown machine, locked-in WR1 ceiling
6801.body:
Higgins keeps producing like a top-tier wideout on a complementary target diet. In 2025 he posted 0.356 EPA per play and a 54.1% success rate across just 15 games, turning 98 targets and a 16.1% target share into 846 yards and 11 touchdowns. The volume sits behind Ja'Marr Chase, but the efficiency is loud: 1.65 yards per route, 27.9% air-yards share, and 15 red-zone looks that fuel his scoring. He drops almost nothing, with two on the year and a 2% drop rate.

The 2024 line is the dream upside, when 0.526 EPA per play and a 63.3% success rate carried 73 catches in 12 games. As a high-end WR2 with WR1 weeks when Burrow is upright, he's a clean Round 3-4 anchor. The one flag is leaning on touchdowns to mask a modest route-run usage rate; any bump in target share unlocks a real bargain.

6806.name: J.K. Dobbins
6806.headline: Efficient runner, fragile body, touchdown-dependent ceiling
6806.body:
Dobbins keeps producing yards the moment he touches the ball. Across 10 games in 2025 he ripped off 772 rushing yards on 153 carries at 5.05 per pop, with a 13.7% explosive-run rate and four scores before another foot injury ended his year at RB33. His 2.44 yards after contact per attempt and nine broken tackles show the burst is intact; the problem has never been talent. It has been staying on the field.

The concern is volume and pass-game floor. A 2.4% target share and 51.1% snap rate point to an early-down, committee role behind a passing-down complement, so his fantasy value leans heavily on finding the end zone. He flashed a near-neutral -0.005 EPA per play on a thin rushing context, and the 2024 line (905 yards, nine TDs, RB24) is the realistic upside. Treat him as a touchdown-reliant RB2 or flex with real injury risk baked in.

6826.name: Cole Kmet
6826.headline: Kmet's Volume Vanished, And It Isn't Coming Back
6826.body:
The arrow points down, and the route data tells you why. Kmet's 2025 line of 30 catches for 347 yards and 2 scores ranked him just 33rd among tight ends, a steep slide from the 90 targets, 719 yards and TE9 finish he posted in 2023. His target share cratered to 9 percent on a 62.6 route share, and at 0.93 yards per route run he simply isn't earning enough work to matter weekly.

There are flickers worth noting: 0.257 EPA per play and a 47.9 percent success rate mean he was efficient on the limited looks he saw, and he drew six red-zone targets. But the rookie ahead of him has claimed the lead role, capping the ceiling. Treat Kmet as a touchdown-dependent dart throw and a deep-league streamer only, nothing more until the depth chart shifts.

6828.name: AJ Dillon
6828.headline: Dillon's role has vanished, and so has his value
6828.body:
Dillon's 2025 line tells the whole story: six games, an 11.9% snap share, 12 carries, and a 0.6% target share that left him with just 6.1 fantasy points. The efficiency wasn't there to justify more, either, with a brutal minus-0.29 EPA per play. Even the surface 5.0 yards per carry is noise off a 12-attempt sample, and a fumble on only three rushing chances per broken tackle hardly screams trusted back.

Go back to his last real workload in 2023, when 178 carries produced only 3.44 yards a pop and two scores across 15 games, and the ceiling looks just as capped. Now in Carolina, he projects as a touchdown-dependent committee piece buried on the depth chart, with usage that may not even clear RB2 duties. There's no realistic path to fantasy relevance here unless an injury ahead of him forces volume his way. Leave him on waivers.

6847.name: Devin Duvernay
6847.headline: Return Specialist, Not a Fantasy Asset
6847.body:
Duvernay's offensive footprint has all but vanished. Across 17 games in 2025 he logged just a 3.5% snap rate and 4.2% route share, drawing five targets for a 0.9% target share and turning two catches into 26 yards. His marks sat firmly underwater: a minus-0.062 EPA per play, a 40% success rate, and 1.04 yards per route run, all good for just 2.6 fantasy points and a WR192 finish. That follows a 2024 in Jacksonville where 16 targets yielded 79 yards, confirming the trend rather than bucking it.

Now in Arizona on a modest one-year deal, he profiles as a kick-returner and special-teams piece rather than a rotational receiver. With no real path to offensive volume, he carries no standalone value, slotting near WR249 in dynasty and going undrafted in redraft. Leave him on the wire unless an injury forces a snap-count shift that simply isn't projected.

6869.name: Adam Trautman
6869.headline: A blocker first, Trautman stays off fantasy radars
6869.body:
Trautman's profile screams in-line blocker. He ran only 270 routes on a 42.5% route share in 2025, drawing 23 targets for a 3.9% target share and a tiny 0.085 targets per route run. That funneled into 20 catches for 195 yards and one score, good for just 25.5 fantasy points and a TE51 rank. He was efficient when used (87% catch rate, 65.2% success rate, 0.469 EPA per play), but the volume simply isn't there, and his 0.72 yards per route run confirms it.

Nothing in the snapshot suggests a breakout. A fresh three-year extension locks in his blocking role, but Evan Engram owns the receiving work and two rookie tight ends add depth. His target share has actually shrunk from 7.3% in 2023, and red-zone looks dried up to a single trip last year. He's a deep-league afterthought at best.

7075.name: Charlie Woerner
7075.headline: Blocking Specialist, Not A Fantasy Asset
7075.body:
Woerner's 2025 line tells the whole story: 8 catches on 9 targets for 48 yards across 7 games, a 1.7% target share that ranked him 84th among tight ends. His 50.8% snap rate looks respectable until you see it came mostly as a blocker, and a 35.9% route participation that produced just 0.24 yards per route run. The -0.022 EPA per play and 33.3% success rate confirm he moves the needle on the ground, not through the air.

Nothing about the profile points to a 2026 breakout. The 88.9% catch rate is empty efficiency on near-zero volume, with 1.33 air yards per target signaling a checkdown and short-area role. Atlanta values him as an in-line run blocker, and that ceiling caps any fantasy upside. Projections in the 14-reception, one-touchdown neighborhood say it plainly: leave him on the wire in every format.

7083.name: Tyler Huntley
7083.headline: Efficient In Relief, But Buried Behind Lamar
7083.body:
Huntley's 2025 sample back in Baltimore was sharp even if tiny: across five games he completed 77.6% of his throws with a 12.9 CPOE, posted a 49.5% success rate and a positive 0.132 EPA per play, and protected the ball with two touchdowns against zero picks for 40.1 points and a QB49 finish. That marked a clean reversal from his messy 2024 Miami stint, where he managed a minus-0.127 EPA, a 39.7% success rate, three interceptions, and five fumbles.

The efficiency is real, yet the opportunity is not. He sits firmly behind Lamar Jackson on a two-year Baltimore deal and projects for minimal snaps in an offense built around its franchise quarterback. With an ADP near pick 329 and roughly zero rostership, he is a non-factor in standard formats. Stash him only in deep two-quarterback leagues as injury insurance, because any standalone value hinges entirely on Jackson missing time.

7526.name: Jaylen Waddle
7526.headline: Waddle's Efficiency Finally Lands in a Pass-First Offense
7526.body:
Don't let the modest box score fool you: Waddle's 2025 was quietly elite by efficiency. He posted a 0.345 EPA per play and a 56% success rate while commanding a 21.6% target share and a 0.597 WOPR across 16 games. His 2.33 yards per route run and 13.06 air yards per target show a downfield threat who simply lacked enough looks in Miami's run-leaning attack, turning 100 targets into 64 grabs for 910 yards and six scores.

Now he's the centerpiece of Denver's passing game, and that volume jump is the whole thesis. Bo Nix tilts short and intermediate, which fits Waddle's separation profile even if it caps the air-yard ceiling. His three prior 21-plus target-share campaigns all cleared 18.9%, so the role is proven. Injuries remain the lone caveat, but a true No. 1 workload makes a WR2 finish the floor and a breakout entirely live.

7529.name: Gary Brightwell
7529.headline: Roster fringe in Cincinnati with no path to touches
7529.body:
Brightwell's profile is built on the thinnest possible sample. Our last meaningful look came in 2023, when he played just four games and logged only nine carries for 19 yards at a dismal 2.11 per attempt, with an EPA of minus-0.115 per play that screams replacement-level. He flashed marginally as a checkdown outlet, hauling in five of seven targets for 47 yards and a 1.96 yards-per-route-run mark, but a 1.4 percent target share and 6.6 fantasy points underscore how invisible he was. That RB105 finish is the ceiling, not the floor.

Now with the Bengals after bouncing across practice squads, he sits at the very bottom of the depth chart with essentially no draftable value. Ranked outside RB160 and rostered in roughly zero percent of leagues, Brightwell is a special-teams body and emergency depth, not a fantasy asset. Leave him on waivers until injuries force a real conversation.

7553.name: Kyle Pitts
7553.headline: Pitts Reclaims TE2 Status As Atlanta's Clear No. 2
7553.body:
The breakout finally arrived. Pitts ranked second among our tight ends in 2025, posting 88 grabs on 118 targets for 928 yards and five scores across all 17 games. His 22.7% target share and 0.217 targets per route run dwarfed the 13.7% and 0.161 marks from a year prior, and the efficiency followed: a 0.216 EPA per play, 55.9% success rate, 1.71 yards per route run, and a 0.487 WOPR that screams featured weapon. He ran a route on 95.8% of dropbacks, so the volume is locked in.

With thin receiver depth behind Drake London, Pitts profiles as the clear second option, and a new tight-end-friendly scheme should funnel even more short, efficient looks his way. The only red flags are touchdown ceiling, where five remains a career best, and lingering trade chatter after the franchise tender. At a Round 6 to 7 cost and a TE8 consensus, the value is plain.

7564.name: Ja'Marr Chase
7564.headline: Ja'Marr Chase: elite WR1 floor, Burrow-driven ceiling
7564.body:
Chase still graded as our WR4 in 2025, posting 125 catches on a commanding 185 targets for 1,412 yards and eight scores across 16 games, good for 188.6 fantasy points. The dip in efficiency was telling, though: his EPA per play fell from 0.44 to 0.224 and his success rate slid from 61.7% to 53%, almost entirely a function of unstable quarterback play rather than any decline in his game. His 30.4% target share actually rose, confirming he remains the unquestioned engine of this passing attack.

The blueprint sits one year back, when a healthy Joe Burrow fueled a 276-point, 17-touchdown campaign that finished as our overall WR1. Restore that battery and Chase's red-zone volume and yards-after-catch translate into league-winning weeks again. Consensus already treats him as a top-three overall pick and the position's premier option, and that price is justified. Draft him without hesitation as a foundational WR1 whose only real variable is Burrow's availability.

7571.name: Rashod Bateman
7571.headline: Bateman's 2025 Cratered, And The Numbers Are Brutal
7571.body:
The advanced profile fell off a cliff in 2025. Bateman managed just 224 yards and two scores across 13 games, his target share collapsing to 9.3% on 38 looks while his catch rate dropped to a dismal 50%. His efficiency cratered alongside the volume: a minus-0.072 EPA per play, a 44.7% success rate, and a meager 0.76 yards per route run that ranked him WR110. That is a near-total erosion of the WR38 form he flashed in 2024.

The 2024 baseline is the reason to keep watching: 756 yards, nine touchdowns, a 1.83 YPRR, and a 0.625 EPA per play on a 15.8% target share. But that ceiling now sits behind Zay Flowers, with rookies pushing for snaps and the role looking like a downfield complement rather than a featured target. He is a deep-league dart throw until the volume returns.

7588.name: Javonte Williams
7588.headline: Williams secures Dallas backfield after career-best Cowboys debut
7588.body:
Williams reset his value in 2025, rumbling for 1,201 yards on 252 carries at 4.77 per tote with 11 rushing scores across 16 games, good for 207.8 fantasy points and an RB15 finish. The volume was elite: 69% snaps, 66 red-zone opportunities, 20 goal-line carries, plus 35 grabs on 51 targets. He forced 25 broken tackles, gained 2.39 yards after contact, and ripped six runs of 20-plus with a long of 66, a leap from his 3.69 YPC Denver swan song.

His -0.035 EPA per play still trails the position's best, and the 8.4% target share caps full-PPR appeal, but Dallas stood pat behind him and handed him a new deal. As the locked-in lead back drafted around the third-fourth round turn, Williams profiles as a sturdy RB2 with weekly RB1 ceiling if the early-season touchdown pace holds.

7594.name: Chuba Hubbard
7594.headline: The 2024 breakout cratered; can Hubbard rebuild value?
7594.body:
The 2025 tape is hard to defend. Hubbard slid to RB41 on 95.4 fantasy points across 15 games, with his yards per carry collapsing from 4.78 to 3.81 and his snap share falling from 77.3% to just 45.8%. He managed one rushing touchdown after scoring 10 the year prior, broke a single tackle on 134 carries, and posted zero breakaway runs while his EPA per play stayed underwater at -0.059. Yards after contact dipped to 1.81, and red-zone work dried up to 24 opportunities from 56.

There is a path back. The 2024 baseline showed a genuine bell-cow capable of 26 carries of 10-plus yards and double-digit scores, and the receiving role held steady at 30 grabs and three touchdowns. But job security is the real question, with younger backs pushing for snaps. Treat him as a mid-round dart throw whose ceiling depends entirely on reclaiming volume.

7595.name: Tylan Wallace
7595.headline: Special-teams body with a deep-ball lottery ticket attached
7595.body:
The profile speaks for itself: across 11 games in 2025, Wallace ran just 78 routes, drew 8 targets, and converted a 2% target share into 4 catches for 45 yards and a score. A 16.8% route rate and 0.58 yards per route run confirm a player who barely sees the field on offense, even if his 0.189 EPA per play kept him modestly efficient on tiny volume.

The ceiling lives in 2024, when 11 grabs turned into 193 yards, a 17.6 yards-per-reception clip, an 84-yard explosive, a 75% success rate, and a sparkling 1.057 EPA per play. That field-stretching upside is real but unreliable. Now in Cleveland after his Baltimore run, he profiles as a return specialist and depth flier rather than a roster lock. With no projectable target volume, he stays off fantasy radars outside the deepest dynasty stashes.

7597.name: Kenny Yeboah
7597.headline: Yeboah's Cardinals Audition Starts From The Bottom
7597.body:
Yeboah has barely cracked the field across his career, logging just 19% of snaps over seven games in 2024 and running only 41 routes. The flashes exist when he plays: a 0.34 EPA per play, a 50% success rate, and 1.15 yards per route run all hint at a competent role player when targeted. But the sample is microscopic, anchored by a 1.4% target share, five receptions, 47 yards, and one touchdown. A 25% drop rate on his limited looks is the glaring red flag, and his 0.195 targets per route run can't be trusted at that volume.

Now in Arizona, he arrives as a tryout signing fighting for a reserve job in a crowded tight end room. There's no defined path to snaps, no ADP, and effectively zero roster relevance. Treat him as a deep-league dart throw only if camp news points toward a real special-teams or blocking role.

7670.name: Joshua Palmer
7670.headline: Buffalo's Field-Stretcher Hunts a Bigger 2026 Slice
7670.body:
Palmer's first Buffalo season was a quiet one on paper, but the underlying work hints at upside. Across 12 games he ran routes on just 39.7% of dropbacks, drew a 7.7% target share, and turned 37 looks into 22 grabs for 303 yards. The downfield role survived the move: 11.46 air yards per target and a 19.3 air-yards-per-reception mark rank among the deepest on the roster, and he posted a healthy 0.182 EPA per play with a 43.2% success rate despite zero touchdowns.

The story is opportunity, not ability. His 2024 baseline in Los Angeles, a 13.2% target share, 0.388 EPA per play, and 52.3% success rate, shows what fuller usage unlocks. With six red-zone targets already and consistent vertical separation, even a modest snap bump pushes him into flex relevance. He is a cheap late-round dart with real spike-week equity in a high-volume passing attack.

7694.name: Tommy Tremble
7694.headline: Efficient on a sliver of the targets
7694.body:
Tremble finished as our TE47 in 2025, and the profile reads like a part-time tight end who does the little he gets well. Across 15 games he ran 283 routes but drew just 37 targets, a 7.7% share and a thin 0.131 targets per route run, turning that volume into 27 grabs for 249 yards, 2 scores and 36.9 fantasy points. The efficiency is real: a 0.223 EPA per play, a 56.8% success rate and a 73% catch rate all rank well, but at 0.88 yards per route run the ceiling is capped.

The blocking-first usage hasn't budged in three seasons, with route rates stuck near 50% and target shares hovering around 6 to 8%. Carolina splits tight end snaps, so meaningful volume only arrives if the room thins out. Draft him as a deep-league dart, not a weekly starter.

7812.name: Simi Fehoko
7812.headline: Blocking Specialist With No Path To Targets
7812.body:
Fehoko's 2024 profile screams deep-roster afterthought: 16 targets across six games for a 3.3% target share, a 0.132 rate per route run, and just six catches on 37.5% efficiency. The 106 yards came on a vertical diet, 14.75 air yards per look, yet only 0.88 yards per route run and a WOPR under 0.09 confirm he never commanded volume. A 0.168 EPA per play and 37.5% success rate read fine in a tiny sample, but two of his eight catchable balls hit the ground.

Now in Arizona on a one-year deal, the 6-foot-4 Stanford product profiles as a blocker and special-teams body rather than a rotational pass-catcher. He logged zero scores in 2024 after his lone 2023 receiving touchdown, and carries no meaningful ADP or ranking. Until injuries clear a path, treat him as roster filler with negligible fantasy upside in any format.

8110.name: Jake Ferguson
8110.headline: Ferguson's volume floor is real, but the ceiling is capped
8110.body:
Ferguson turned a healthy 17 games and a 77.5% route rate into 102 targets, 82 catches, and 8 scores in 2025, his TD output rebounding from a goose egg the year prior. The efficiency, though, tells the cap story: 7.32 yards per reception, just 1.19 yards per route run, and an EPA per play of 0.177 that trailed his 2023 peak of 0.321. A 16.8% target share and 24 red-zone looks anchor the floor, but a 47.1% success rate and microscopic 4.66 air yards per target frame him as a possession piece, not a field-stretcher.

Behind CeeDee Lamb and George Pickens, he stays third in the pecking order, leaning on red-zone work and Dak Prescott's availability. His one collapse came when Prescott missed time. With elite hands (just two drops, 80.4% catch rate) and consistent six-target volume, treat him as a high-end TE2 worth a double-digit-round flier.

8112.name: Drake London
8112.headline: London's Target Hog Profile Survives Any Quarterback
8112.body:
Even in an injury-shortened 2025, London soaked up a 21.6% target share and ran 0.294 targets per route, posting a 0.212 EPA per play and a 53.6% success rate across 12 games. The fuller picture is the 2024 ceiling: 158 looks, a 29.3% target share, 0.71 WOPR and nine scores while finishing the WR4 in advanced rank. He buys you 15 red-zone targets in a partial season and barely drops anything, with a 0.9% drop rate on 69 catchable balls.

The volume travels regardless of who lines up under center, with Tagovailoa now pushing Penix and London entrenched as the unquestioned focal point. Drafters are paying up around the WR7 range and a top-20 overall slot, which is fair for a player carrying a near-30% air-yards share. Health is the only real variable; the usage and efficiency are already WR1-caliber when he's on the field.

8119.name: Jahan Dotson
8119.headline: Dotson lands in Atlanta hunting a real target share
8119.body:
Two years buried behind A.J. Brown and DeVonta Smith left almost nothing on Dotson's profile. In 2025 he ran 376 routes for just 36 targets, a 7.8% share, and 18 catches for 262 yards and a single score. The efficiency was brutal: 0.7 yards per route run, a 33.3% success rate, and minus-0.418 EPA per play, ranking him 104th. He stayed on the field for 15 games but the role never materialized.

The upside lives in 2023, when Washington gave him 83 targets, a 13.8% share, and 49 grabs for 518 yards and four touchdowns. A two-year Atlanta deal reopens that door, but he is one of several bodies brought in to replace Darnell Mooney inside a run-leaning offense. Until he separates from that committee, treat him as a late-round dart and bench depth, not a weekly starter. ADP near WR125 reflects exactly that.

8130.name: Trey McBride
8130.headline: The undisputed TE1, even with touchdown regression looming
8130.body:
McBride isn't a tight end so much as a featured No. 1 receiver who happens to line up inline. He posted a 27.4% target share on 169 looks in 2025, ran a route on 94.1% of dropbacks, and turned that volume into 126 catches for 1,239 yards. The underlying efficiency backs the workload: a 0.436 EPA per play, a 60.4% success rate, and just two drops on 128 catchable balls. He's been a target magnet for three straight years, and at 25 the usage floor is rock solid.

The only real caution is the 11 touchdowns, which more than quintupled his 2024 total of two and won't fully repeat. Even halved, his receiving profile keeps him in the elite tier, and his 33 red-zone targets argue he'll stay involved near the goal line. A new coaching staff and added backfield investment could trim Arizona's league-leading pass volume, but no tight end pairs this target share with this efficiency. Draft him as the position's clear top option.

8132.name: Tyler Allgeier
8132.headline: Touchdown vulture chasing carries in a crowded Arizona backfield
8132.body:
Allgeier's 2025 line tells the story of a goal-line specialist, not a featured back. He punched in eight rushing scores on just 143 carries while averaging a modest 3.59 yards per attempt, and his EPA per play sat underwater at -0.055. The volume was concentrated near the stripe: 33 red-zone opportunities and 10 goal-line carries propped up a profile that managed only a 30.2% snap share and a 3.1% target share. That formula finished RB44 with 109 points, and the 1.61 yards after contact per attempt confirms the efficiency ceiling is low.

The move to Arizona muddies things further. He now sits behind James Conner, rookie Jeremiyah Love, and Trey Benson, with passing downs spoken for. Drafters are treating him as a late dart at RB48 ADP. Bank on touchdown poaching and injury-contingent upside rather than steady weekly snaps, and keep him on the bench until the depth chart clears.

8134.name: Khalil Shakir
8134.headline: Reliable Floor, Capped Ceiling as Moore Crowds the Room
8134.body:
Shakir's 2025 line tells a clear story: 72 catches on 95 targets for 719 yards and four scores across 16 games, a 75.8% catch rate, and a tidy 2.1% drop rate. The underlying marks softened from his 2024 peak, with EPA per play falling from 0.442 to 0.099 and success rate sliding from 57% to 44.2%. His 19.8% target share and 0.27 targets per route held steady, but a 3.67 air-yards-per-target number confirms a short-area role doing most of its damage at 7.51 yards after the catch.

That profile screams dependable possession option, not breakout. D.J. Moore's arrival threatens Shakir's standing as Buffalo's target leader and likely trims his volume closer to five looks a game. With limited downfield and red-zone equity, the ceiling stays modest. Treat him as a steady WR4 with a high floor, drafted around the WR53-54 range, rather than chasing untapped upside.

8137.name: George Pickens
8137.headline: Pickens' Dallas Breakout Is Real, Not a Mirage
8137.body:
Pickens turned his first year in Dallas into a top-3 finish in our WR model, hauling in 93 of 137 targets for 1,429 yards and nine scores across all 17 games. The efficiency drove it: a 0.64 EPA per play and a 63.5% success rate dwarfed his Pittsburgh marks (0.27 and 41.7% in 2024), while 2.46 yards per route and a 0.235 target-per-route rate confirmed he was no longer a boom-or-bust deep threat. A 22.6% target share and 23 red-zone looks, even sharing a backfield-style workload with CeeDee Lamb, gave him a real floor.

The questions are off the field. Pickens is angling for a new deal and skipped voluntary work, and a sturdier Dallas defense could trim pass volume. Still, he profiles as a back-end WR1 going around the second-round turn, and his per-game ceiling justifies that price.

8138.name: James Cook
8138.headline: Cook's career-best volume makes him a locked-in RB1
8138.body:
Cook took a leap in 2025, finishing as our RB4 on 269.2 fantasy points while leading the league with 1,621 rushing yards on 309 carries at a punishing 5.25 per tote. His efficiency held under that heavy workload: a 12.62% explosive-run rate, nine carries of 20-plus yards, 21 broken tackles and 2.22 yards after contact per attempt. He also tucked away 12 rushing scores plus 16 goal-line carries, so the touchdown equity is real even if 2024's gaudy 16 TDs was always going to settle down.

The lone soft spot is the passing game. A 1.17 YPRR, 8.4% target share and just 33 catches leave value on the table for a back this dynamic in space. If Buffalo finally leans into him on third downs, his ceiling climbs. With no new competition in the backfield and elite rushing volume guaranteed, Cook is a safe early-round anchor.

8147.name: John Metchie
8147.headline: Metchie's Carolina move can't outrun his career ceiling
8147.body:
Four years in, Metchie still hasn't cleared the role-player tier, and 2025 confirmed it: 13 games, a 46.1% snap share, 48 targets on a 10.3% target share, and just 274 yards on 33 catches. The efficiency markers are the bigger red flag. His 8.3 yards per reception and 1.16 yards per route run are replacement-level, his minus-0.049 EPA per play dipped back below water after a brief 2024 uptick, and a 43.8% success rate offers nothing to dream on. Two touchdowns and 38.9 fantasy points across a full slate is the production floor of fantasy irrelevance.

Carolina signed him to a one-year deal, and the depth-chart math is unforgiving behind the established top of their receiver room. A WR125 consensus rank and an ADP outside the top 150 receivers price him correctly. Without a target-share leap he has never shown, Metchie is a late-camp watch, not a draftable asset.

8154.name: Brian Robinson
8154.headline: From feature back to forgotten man
8154.body:
The 2025 collapse is hard to overstate: Robinson cratered to RB56 on just 92 carries across 17 games, his snap share gutted to 16.7% after a midseason move. The 400 rushing yards and two scores barely cleared 54 fantasy points, and a minus-0.046 EPA per play confirmed he was a passenger, not a driver. Even his efficiency markers thinned, with 1.77 yards after contact per attempt and a 4.35-yard average that flattered light volume.

Rewind only one year and the contrast stings. In 2024 he was an RB27 workhorse on 187 carries, 51.6% of snaps, eight touchdowns and 46 red-zone looks. That early-down, goal-line profile still exists, but his receiving role has shriveled from a 1.36 YPRR peak in 2023 to 0.37 last season. He needs a clear path to opportunity, and right now Atlanta's backfield does not promise one. Treat him as a late-round bench dart.

8160.name: Kenny Pickett
8160.headline: A Veteran Insurance Policy Behind Bryce Young
8160.body:
Pickett's 2025 cameo in Las Vegas confirmed what his profile keeps suggesting: a steady hand who doesn't move the needle. Across six appearances he completed 62.2% of his throws for just 188 yards, one touchdown against two picks, and a brutal -0.552 EPA per play with a 29.3% success rate. That finished him 61st among quarterbacks at 7.7 fantasy points, a far cry from the QB29 ranking and 39.8% success rate he posted as Pittsburgh's starter in 2023.

Now on his third team in three years, Pickett lands in Carolina as the clear backup behind Bryce Young on a roster trending upward. The one-year deal is depth insurance, nothing more. There's no draftable path here outside Superflex or two-quarterback formats, and even then only as a contingency stash should Young miss time. Leave him on the waiver wire and monitor the situation.

8207.name: Tyler Goodson
8207.headline: Deep handcuff with no path to volume
8207.body:
Goodson's profile screams emergency depth, not draftable asset. In 2025 he logged just 10 games on a 5% snap share, turning nine carries into 24 yards at 2.67 per rush with a brutal -0.327 EPA per play and only 3.2 fantasy points. Even his stronger 2024 line, 153 rushing yards on 4.78 a pop with a positive 0.085 EPA across 14 games, came with a 12.6% snap rate and a 3% target share, the ceiling of a clear backup.

The 2026 setup does nothing to change that math. He sits behind Jonathan Taylor with a role projected to stay minimal, an ECR around 456 overall and an RB rank near 119, and he is rostered in roughly zero leagues. A groin issue has trended the right way, but there is no realistic standalone value here. Goodson is purely a contingency stash, only relevant if the workhorse ahead of him misses extended time.

8249.name: Lucas Krull
8249.headline: Depth Tight End Without A Path To Volume
8249.body:
Krull's profile screams roster filler. His healthiest stretch came in 2024, when 10 games and 200 routes produced just 23 targets, a 4.2% target share, and 152 yards on 19 catches. The efficiency markers were quietly fine, an 82.6% catch rate, +0.195 EPA per play, a 52.2% success rate, and 0.76 yards per route run, but the volume never scaled. His 2025 was a non-event: two games, four targets, a 0.7% share, and 1.5 fantasy points before disappearing entirely.

Nothing about the role suggests a leap. He re-signed in Denver on a one-year deal buried in the tight end room, sitting outside the top 150 in early consensus and rostered in essentially zero leagues. The career arc, one touchdown across three seasons and a peak of 152 yards, caps the ceiling hard. Until injuries above him force snaps, Krull is a deep-dynasty stash and nothing more in redraft.

8423.name: Brittain Brown
8423.headline: Big-Play Flashes Buried On Chicago's Practice Squad
8423.body:
Brown's 2025 sample was tiny but eye-catching: 5 carries for 37 yards, a 7.4 average, a touchdown, and a 22-yard burst that accounted for one explosive run on 20% of his attempts. His 0.405 EPA per play and 4.6 yards after contact per attempt signal a back who creates when handed space, and he forced two missed tackles on five totes for a 40% elude rate. The catch: just two games, a 6.7% snap share, and one target.

The profile is intriguing, the path is not. Brown sits on Chicago's practice squad purely for depth, and at 27 he carries no real draft value, landing outside the top 120 backs in consensus and rostered in essentially zero leagues. Until injuries clear the rotation ahead of him, this is a watch-list name whose efficiency markers only matter if opportunity ever arrives. For now, leave him on waivers.

8800.name: Malik Davis
8800.headline: Deep-bench flier whose efficiency outran his volume
8800.body:
Davis touched the ball just 52 times on the ground across 10 games in 2025, yet he made those carries count: 250 rushing yards at 4.81 per tote, two scores, and a 43-yard breakaway that doubled as his longest touchdown. Roughly 8 percent of his runs went for explosive or breakaway gains, and his 2.96 yards before contact hints the blocking, not the back, capped his ceiling. The drag was a 20.7 percent snap share, a 0.8 percent target share, and a minus-0.07 EPA per play that pulled his fantasy total to 38.6.

The 2026 path is opportunity, not talent. Behind Javonte Williams, Davis is fighting for backup reps that could vanish to younger legs, and a late-season calf and eye injury didn't help his standing. He's a final-round dart in deep leagues, worth a flier only if camp anoints him the clear handcuff.

8917.name: KaVontae Turpin
8917.headline: Big-play juice, but the volume just isn't there
8917.body:
Turpin's 2025 efficiency pops in isolation: a 0.44 EPA per play, a 52.6 percent success rate and a 15.2-yard average on his 26 grabs, capped by an 86-yard burst. The problem is everything underneath. He ran a route on just 35.9 percent of Dallas dropbacks, drew a 6.3 percent target share and turned 38 looks into only 396 yards and two scores, finishing 70th at the position. His 1.69 yards per route run actually slid backward from 2024's 2.09 as the snaps thinned out.

That profile screams big-play gadget, not weekly starter. The route count caps his ceiling, and the back-end ECR and triple-digit WR ADP reflect a player buried on the depth chart. Unless the role meaningfully expands, his real fantasy value lives in return-yardage formats, where the field-flipping speed finally translates to points. Leave him on waivers in standard leagues.

9221.name: Jahmyr Gibbs
9221.headline: Gibbs profiles as a true bell-cow ascendant
9221.body:
Gibbs has built one of the steadiest profiles at the position, ranking RB5 in our 2025 model after posting RB3 (310.9 points) in 2024. Last season he stacked 289.9 fantasy points across 17 games, splitting work as a runner and receiver: 243 carries for 1,223 yards at 5.03 a pop with 13 rushing scores, plus 77 grabs on 94 targets for 616 yards and five more touchdowns. The receiving floor is rare, and his red-zone opportunity total anchors the touchdown equity.

If the early-down committee that has capped his carries thins out, a path opens toward a true workhorse share in a high-powered Detroit attack. His passing-down role already spiked down the stretch, pushing a 17.1% target share that should climb further. Goal-line and red-zone volume both project up, and the dual-threat usage makes him a defensible first-overall selection in PPR formats.

9224.name: Chase Brown
9224.headline: Three-Down Bengal Workhorse Primed For A Full-Season Leap
9224.body:
Brown's 2025 ledger reads like a foundational fantasy asset: an RB9 finish on 213.6 points across 17 games, with 232 carries for 1,019 yards (4.39 per pop), six rushing scores, and a genuine receiving floor of 69 grabs for 437 yards plus five touchdowns. The 14.5% target share and 47 red-zone opportunities, including 12 goal-line carries, confirm Cincinnati funnels its highest-value touches through him.

The efficiency tells the only cautionary tale, a slightly negative minus-0.046 EPA per play and modest 1.97 yards after contact, but Brown's profile spiked dramatically once Joe Burrow returned, and his usage never wavered. Drafters are paying up accordingly, with a top-20 overall ADP and an RB11 price tag. A full slate behind a healthy Burrow points squarely at career-best volume and clear top-tier upside in 2026.

9228.name: Bryce Young
9228.headline: Career highs, but the efficiency still says backup
9228.body:
Young set personal bests in 2025 with 23 touchdowns, 3,011 yards and a 63.6% completion rate across 16 games, yet the underlying profile barely budged. His EPA per play sat at -0.03 for the second straight year, his success rate ticked up only to 42.6%, and a finish as our QB21 tells you the volume never arrived. A 7.7% deep-attempt rate and 4.8 completed air yards per completion confirm an offense that lived underneath, and 11 picks against modest production cap the ceiling.

There are flickers of growth: CPOE climbed to +1.0, the bad-throw rate fell to 16.4%, and on-target percentage rose to 77.9%. But 216 rushing yards and two scores add little, and the half-season of sub-25-attempt games signals a coaching staff still managing him. Treat Young as a superflex-only stash with real bust risk, not a one-quarterback option.

9493.name: Puka Nacua
9493.headline: Puka Nacua's elite efficiency makes him a foundational WR1
9493.body:
Nacua sits as the No. 2 receiver in our advanced model after a dominant 2025: 129 catches on 166 targets for 1,715 yards and 10 touchdowns across 16 games, anchored by a 28.6% target share and a league-leading 3.71 yards per route run. The supporting metrics are absurd, a 0.697 EPA per play and a 70.5% success rate, and his 246 fantasy points led the position. Even his injury-shortened 2024, just 11 games and 990 yards, came with a career-high target rate, proving the volume share never wavers.

The Rams funnel everything through him, and with Matthew Stafford back under center he projects as the clear primary read once again. That floor explains why he carries a top-four consensus value and belongs in the opening tier of any draft. Health is the only real variable; the per-game production is too elite to fade, so treat him as a cornerstone pick.

9494.name: Marvin Mims
9494.headline: Big-Play Gadget Talent Still Stuck Behind a Crowded Room
9494.body:
Mims took a step back in 2025, finishing as our WR86 with a tepid 0.069 EPA per play and a 41.2% success rate while logging just a 36.9% snap rate across 15 games. His 8.7% target share and 0.199 targets per route run translated to 37 catches for 322 yards and one score, and the 1.26 yards per route run badly undersold the explosive profile he flashed a year earlier (0.449 EPA, 2.76 YPRR, six touchdowns). The efficiency cratered as the role stayed part-time.

Denver's offseason did him no favors. Adding Jaylen Waddle behind Courtland Sutton pushes Mims into a target competition with Troy Franklin and Pat Bryant for whatever scraps remain. His return work keeps him relevant in special-teams scoring leagues, but as a pure receiver he profiles as a late-round dart and bench stash whose ceiling hinges on an injury opening reps ahead of him.

9509.name: Bijan Robinson
9509.headline: Bijan Robinson is a locked-in 2026 RB1 anchor
9509.body:
Robinson graded as our overall RB1 in 2025, banking 291.8 fantasy points across all 17 games on a near-bell-cow snap share. He paired a heavy rushing workload with a 19.8% target share, 79 catches and 820 receiving yards, layering elite receiving production onto the ground game. That caps a steady climb from RB6 (188.3) as a rookie to RB4 (280.7) in 2024 to the top of the board in 2025.

The only real knock is touchdown variance; his rushing scores dipped even as total volume rose, a number that should normalize so long as the goal-line work stays largely his. The dual-threat floor is bulletproof regardless of game script, which is exactly what you want from a foundational pick. Drafted around the top two overall league-wide, he carries genuine RB1-overall upside if Atlanta finds the end zone more often.

9997.name: Zay Flowers
9997.headline: Flowers Blooms Into a Locked-In WR1 Target
9997.body:
Flowers graded out as our WR6 in 2025, and the workload backs it up: a 29% target share, a 0.681 WOPR, and 118 looks across all 17 games. He converted that into 86 grabs for 1,211 yards on a 72.9% catch rate, while his 2.75 yards per route run and 0.401 EPA per play put him among the most efficient receivers in football. His 57.6% success rate confirms this wasn't volume noise.

The one cap is the end zone. Just 11 red zone targets and 5 end zone looks held him to five scores, and he's never topped that mark. That keeps the ceiling tethered to Baltimore's run-leaning script and Lamar Jackson's touchdown rate. But the route volume, the air-yards share north of 35%, and a third straight rising target tree make him a steady early-round WR2 with genuine WR1 weeks baked in.

10225.name: Jonathan Mingo
10225.headline: Mingo's collapse leaves nothing for fantasy to chase
10225.body:
The arrow points straight down. Mingo's positional finish has cratered from 76th in 2023 to 124th in 2024 to 194th this past season, when his role evaporated to a 20.7% snap share across just five games. He drew five targets total, an 0.8% team share, and converted them into one catch for 25 yards. His -0.84 EPA per play and 20% success rate weren't small-sample noise so much as confirmation of a 2024 that already bottomed out at a 28.6% success rate.

The context is just as bleak. He sits at the back of a crowded Dallas receiver room with no path to meaningful snaps, and a restructured deal stripping his workout bonus signals how the team values him. He's effectively unrostered league-wide and projects for minimal volume. There is no reason to draft him in any standard format.

10226.name: Andrei Iosivas
10226.headline: Steady WR3 snaps, but the touchdowns dried up
10226.body:
Iosivas locked down the No. 3 role in 2025, playing 76.2% of snaps across 15 games and running a route on nearly 80% of dropbacks. The volume was real but capped: a 9.5% target share, 58 looks, 0.81 yards per route, and a 13.18 average produced just 33-435-2. His 0.201 EPA per play and 50% success rate flag an efficient complementary piece, yet that WR66 finish reflects a clear ceiling behind Cincinnati's top two.

The alarming dip is in the end zone. After six scores on 10 red-zone and 10 end-zone targets in 2024, those high-value looks collapsed to seven and four, dragging his touchdowns to two. With Chase and Higgins commanding the bulk of work, Iosivas remains a snap-secure but low-equity bench stash whose weekly value spikes only when an injury opens targets ahead of him.

10228.name: Charlie Jones
10228.headline: Special-teamer with no offensive runway in Cincinnati
10228.body:
Three seasons in, Jones still hasn't carved out an offensive role, and the trend is going the wrong direction. He played 14 games in 2025 but logged just 11 routes on a 2.1% snap share, drawing a single target for a 0.2% target share and a brutal -0.534 EPA per play. The 2024 line was nearly identical: 16 routes, one catch for five yards across seven appearances. His career-best workload remains 2023, when he ran 30 routes and turned nine targets into 64 yards at 2.13 yards per route run.

That 2023 efficiency hints at usable hands, but it came on a sample too thin to project anything. An ankle issue ended his 2025 early, and he spent the bulk of the year on special teams behind a crowded Bengals receiver room. Until injuries or departures clear a path, Jones is a roster-clogging zero with no draftable value in any format.

10231.name: Elijah Higgins
10231.headline: McBride's Shadow Keeps Higgins A Deep-League Lottery Ticket
10231.body:
