Higgins quietly logged his most productive season in 2025, suiting up for all 17 games and turning 37 targets into 30 grabs for 301 yards while securing 81.1% of his looks and posting a clean zero-drop sheet. The volume stayed thin, though: a 6% target share, 0.128 targets per route, and 1.04 yards per route run dragged his EPA per play to a flat 0.039 and capped him as the TE38 in fantasy scoring. The encouraging wrinkle is his 6.97 yards after the catch per reception and a 16.7% avoided-tackle rate, signaling he can create when fed.

The ceiling problem is the man in front of him. Higgins remains the clear backup behind Trey McBride, and as long as the starter stays upright there simply aren't enough routes to matter. He's a touchdown-light, low-floor name buried near the end of draft boards. Stash him only in the deepest formats as injury insurance.

10232.name: Michael Wilson
10232.headline: Wilson's 2025 leap leans hard on Harrison's absences
10232.body:
Wilson's 2025 was a genuine ascension on paper: 17 games, 126 targets, a 20.4% target share and a 0.532 WOPR that vaulted him to WR17 with 142.6 fantasy points. The efficiency backs it up, with 1,006 yards, a 1.68 yards-per-route-run mark, 0.212 EPA per play and a 50.8% success rate. He commanded volume too, drawing a 32.2% air-yards share and 16 red-zone looks while converting seven scores and dropping just one of 79 catchable balls.

The catch is context. Much of that production stacked up when Arizona's top option was sidelined, and across 2023-2024 his target shares sat at 10.7% and 13.6% with far thinner output. A healthy pecking order, plus McBride and a new ground-game investment, caps his ceiling. The 11.92 air yards per target and downfield role keep him relevant, but treat the WR17 finish as a soft projection, not a floor, and draft accordingly.

10235.name: Roschon Johnson
10235.headline: Buried Behind Monangai With No Path To Volume
10235.body:
Johnson's trajectory has gone the wrong direction every season. After a promising rookie year (81 carries, 4.35 yards per clip, a 34-catch receiving role and an RB48 finish), his usage cratered to a 2.73 mark on 55 totes in 2024, then collapsed entirely in 2025: six games, two carries, 1.7 fantasy points and a rank of 128. The 8.5 yards-per-carry and 0.679 EPA on that microscopic sample mean nothing without volume.

The job he was hyped to inherit went to seventh-round rookie Kyle Monangai, who locked down the clear backfield No. 2 while Johnson battled injuries. That leaves Johnson a distant third option with no realistic route to touches behind an entrenched, younger back. His goal-line work was the only fantasy hook (six rushing scores in 2024), and even that has dried up. He is waiver-wire depth at best and undraftable in standard formats this year.

10236.name: Dalton Kincaid
10236.headline: Elite Efficiency, Capped Volume: Kincaid's Frustrating Middle Ground
10236.body:
Kincaid was wildly efficient in his 12 games last season, posting 0.823 EPA per play and a 63.3% success rate while finishing TE16. On just 49 targets he caught 39 balls (79.6%) for 571 yards at 14.64 per grab, and his 2.97 yards per route run jumped from 1.68 the year prior. Five touchdowns and eight red-zone looks show the scoring chops are real when he's healthy and on the field.

The problem is the snaps: a 37.4% rate and 10.2% target share that both slid from 2024. With Knox and a rookie eating into the rotation, a target-hungry receiver room, and a knee Buffalo plans to manage, volume is the ceiling-killer here. The per-route metrics scream league-winner, but until the route share climbs back toward 50%, Kincaid is a high-upside TE2 you draft for spikes, not weekly floor.

10444.name: Cedric Tillman
10444.headline: Deep-field flier whose numbers keep saying no
10444.body:
Tillman's 2025 line is a flashing warning light: a 7.5% target share, just 0.13 targets per route, and a 0.90 yards-per-route-run mark that ranks him near WR102 in our model. He converted only 53.8% of those looks into catches for 270 yards and two scores across 12 games, with a 0.198 WOPR that screams complementary, not featured. The lone positive is a 0.167 EPA per play, his best in three years, but a 41% success rate undercuts any efficiency case.

The trajectory hasn't budged either, with his target share parked between 7.4% and 7.7% every season since 2023. A crowded Cleveland room only got tighter after two drafted rookies, capping his snap and air-yards ceiling further. Treat him as a touchdown-dependent dart throw in the deepest formats, nothing more, until a clear path to volume actually opens.

10871.name: Luke Schoonmaker
10871.headline: Buried Behind Ferguson, Schoonmaker Stays Off The Radar
10871.body:
Schoonmaker's 2025 line tells the whole story: 14 games, a 32.9% snap rate, and just 23 targets for a 3.8% share that left him 62nd at the position. He ran only 167 routes, turning them into 14 catches for 132 yards and zero touchdowns at a thin 0.79 yards per route. The 0.158 EPA per play and 43.5% success rate were a clear step back from 2024, when his 0.186 targets per route, 1.24 YPRR, and 5.8% share hinted at more.

The efficiency dip matters because the volume isn't coming. As long as Jake Ferguson owns the position, Schoonmaker is a blocking-tier afterthought drafted around TE67, well outside startable range. He's a Ferguson-injury handcuff and nothing more right now. Until a real route share materializes, there's no reason to roster him in any standard format heading into 2026.

11068.name: Mitch Tinsley
11068.headline: A Deep-Ball Flier With Red-Zone Equity
11068.body:
Tinsley's 2025 line reads like a true field-stretcher: 14.75 air yards per target and a massive 44.25 air yards per reception fed a 0.11 EPA per play across his 24 looks. The problem was conversion. A 33.3% catch rate and matching 33.3% success rate left him at just 116 yards and 0.79 yards per route run, and his WR140 finish reflects a profile that lived on volatile downfield shots rather than reliable separation. Eight catches over eight games with one drop is thin, even by deep-threat standards.

The sliver of intrigue is usage near the goal line. Five red-zone targets and one end-zone look turned into two touchdowns and 23.6 fantasy points, so the touchdown equity is real if his snap share climbs from a modest 24.6%. With only a 3.9% target share and a 0.112 WOPR, Tinsley remains a speculative depth piece who needs an opportunity bump to matter in fantasy lineups this season.

11201.name: Blake Whiteheart
11201.headline: Whiteheart is a snap-count blocker, not a fantasy bet
11201.body:
The numbers leave little room for optimism. Across nine games in 2025, Whiteheart ran just 109 routes on an 18% route share, drawing six targets for a 1.1% target share and converting only two of them into four total yards. His -0.621 EPA per play and 0% success rate were among the worst at the position, and a fumble on such limited volume only deepens the concern. The 2024 sample was thinner but cleaner: six grabs for 51 yards and a red-zone touchdown on a 44.4% success rate.

Nothing here points to a usable role. With a sub-24% snap rate and essentially zero air-yards involvement, he profiles as a blocking specialist rather than a receiving threat. Cleveland's tight end pecking order keeps him buried, and his depressed ranking reflects it. Leave him on the wire in every format and revisit only if injuries open real route volume ahead of him.

11256.name: Tyson Bagent
11256.headline: Bagent stays a clipboard handcuff behind Caleb Williams
11256.body:
Bagent's 2025 line is pure garbage time: two appearances, four attempts, 47 yards and 2.6 fantasy points. The peripherals flatter him in that tiny sample, a 0.322 EPA per play with a plus-9.6 CPOE, but four throws tell you nothing actionable. You have to rewind to his 2023 rookie spot start run for real volume, and that stretch was rough, 143 attempts at a minus-0.119 EPA per play with six picks against three scores while finishing the QB44 in points.

With Caleb Williams entrenched as the starter, Bagent stays glued to the bench, going essentially unrostered with no draft cost attached. The only path to relevance is a Williams injury, which could unlock back-end QB2 value, maybe a flash of QB1 upside, inside Ben Johnson's offense. Until then he is a pure contingency stash, not a draftable asset in any standard format.

11433.name: Nate Adkins
11433.headline: Denver's Blocking Tight End Stays Off the Radar
11433.body:
Adkins is a fantasy non-factor, and our numbers spell out why. His 2025 campaign was a step backward: six games, a 32.8% snap share, and just 11 targets that produced a 1.9% target share, six catches, 48 yards and one score. The efficiency cratered, too, as he posted a minus-0.057 EPA per play, a 36.4% success rate, and a brutal 27.3% drop rate while ranking 85th at the position. Even his 0.72 yards per route run flagged him as a blocker first.

The one tease came in 2024, when 14 receptions on 0.73 YPRR carried a 73.3% success rate, a 0.605 EPA, three touchdowns and zero drops. That spike was tiny and tough to bank on. A one-year extension keeps him in Denver, but the role is clear: in-line blocker, not a pass-game asset. Leave him on waivers in all formats this season.

11439.name: Jaleel McLaughlin
11439.headline: Efficient Spark Plug Buried On Denver's Crowded Depth Chart
11439.body:
McLaughlin keeps producing whenever he touches the ball, he just rarely gets the chance. Across eight 2025 games he turned 37 carries into 187 yards at a robust 5.05 per tote, popping a 13.5% explosive-run rate while playing only 16% of snaps and finishing RB75. That tracks his career: a 5.39 mark and RB47 finish back in 2023, plus 4.39 yards a clip over a full 16-game 2024 slate. The juice is real even if the volume never is.

The problem is opportunity, not talent. A slightly negative EPA per play (-0.014) and a 1% target share reflect a clear fourth-back role behind Dobbins, Harvey, and Coleman in Sean Payton's loaded backfield. Consensus pegs him outside the top 90 backs, and that's fair until an injury opens the door. Stash him only in deep dynasty formats as a contingency-value insurance piece, nothing more.

11474.name: Dylan Drummond
11474.headline: Deep on the depth chart, off the radar
11474.body:
Drummond's 2025 rookie sample reads like a developmental redshirt. Across six games he ran just 71 routes for a 12.5% route share, drawing 11 targets and a 2.1% target share while playing only 21% of snaps. The efficiency underneath was alarming: a minus-0.556 EPA per play, a 36.4% success rate, and a brutal 0.59 yards per route run. He turned seven catches into 42 yards, never finding the end zone despite three red-zone looks.

The path forward is opportunity, not production. Atlanta's receiver room dealt with injuries, yet Drummond still couldn't carve out a defined role, and he projects as a back-end depth piece entering 2026. With essentially no ADP and zero roster relevance, he's a watch-list name at best. Until he earns real snaps and route volume, there's nothing here to draft in any format.

11557.name: Joe Milton
11557.headline: Milton's flashes are real, but the runway isn't
11557.body:
The arm talent shows up in the data, but the sample is microscopic. Across four Dallas appearances in 2025, Milton threw for 183 yards on 24 attempts with a 62.5% completion rate, one touchdown against two picks, and a -0.05 EPA per play paired with a 44.8% success rate. His 26.1% bad-throw rate flagged the rough edges, though a 1.6 CPOE and five carries for 50 rushing yards hinted at a higher ceiling. The 2024 New England tape was the dream: 22-of-29 for 241 yards, a 92.6% on-target rate, 17.1 CPOE, and 19.2 fantasy points in one start.

That upside collides with reality in Dallas. Milton is locked in a QB2 fight with Sam Howell behind a healthy Dak Prescott, leaving no clear path to snaps. With an overall ADP near 383 and QB54 positioning, he's a pure deep-dynasty stash, not a 2026 fantasy asset.

11559.name: Michael Penix
11559.headline: Arm Talent Is Real, Efficiency Has to Catch Up
11559.body:
Penix enters his first full season as Atlanta's starter carrying a thin but loud nine-game sample. He finished QB29 in 2025 with 1,982 yards, nine touchdowns and just three picks across nine starts, posting a modest 0.016 EPA per play and a 44.8% success rate. The accuracy still lags: a 60.1% completion rate, minus-3.6 CPOE and a 24% bad-throw rate say his deep-shot profile (12.1% of attempts) outran his ball placement.

The upside lives in the arm. He stacked 26 throws of 20-plus yards and a 69-yard long despite the truncated workload, and his sturdier 2024 cameo flashed a 0.131 EPA per play. With 13 sacks and four fumbles, ball security and protection are the swing factors. Project a full 17, and the volume alone lifts him into low-end QB2 territory with weekly ceiling games baked in. A leap in efficiency is the difference between streamer and starter.

11560.name: Caleb Williams
11560.headline: The Year Two Leap Is Real
11560.body:
Williams turned a shaky rookie campaign into a genuine QB1 breakout, climbing to a seventh-place finish on 316.2 fantasy points across 17 games. The efficiency jump tells the story: his EPA per play flipped from minus-0.057 to a positive 0.074, success rate rose to 43.8 percent, and his touchdown total nearly tripled the value of his rookie year at 27 scores against just seven picks for a franchise-record 3,942 yards. Crucially, sacks collapsed from 68 to 24 as the pocket finally held.

The dual-threat element keeps the floor sturdy, with 383 rushing yards and three scores layered on top of that passing production. A 58.1 percent completion rate and a 13.9 percent deep-attempt mark leave obvious room to grow, but with an elite play-caller and a loaded receiving room around him, betting on another step forward is the easy call here. Draft him as a high-upside QB1.

11563.name: Bo Nix
11563.headline: Volume-Soaked QB1 Floor, Capped Ceiling in Denver
11563.body:
Nix piled up 612 attempts in 2025, the league lead, yet the efficiency stayed modest: a 0.089 EPA per play, 43.9% success rate, and a minus-1.2 CPOE that placed him eighth among our advanced-stat tracking. He cleared 304.8 fantasy points behind 25 passing scores, 11 picks, and just 11.4% of his throws traveling deep, so the ceiling is propped up by sheer pass count rather than splash plays.

The rushing layer keeps him steady, 356 yards and five touchdowns on 83 carries, the kind of floor that survives cold passing stretches. He absorbed only 22 sacks behind a 19.1% pressure rate, and a refreshed receiving corps led by Courtland Sutton plus the addition of Jaylen Waddle should sharpen the completion math. Monitor the offseason ankle, but a back-end QB1 finish remains the realistic baseline.

11570.name: Rasheen Ali
11570.headline: Deep stash banking on a pass-game role
11570.body:
Ali's 2025 line reads like a true backup's: 15 games but just 13% of snaps, seven carries for 24 yards at 3.43 per pop, and a brutal minus-0.25 EPA per play that flags how little space he created. The flicker of intrigue is through the air. He drew 11 targets, caught nine for 68 yards and a score on 0.79 yards per route run, and his 83 receiving yards after the catch hint at usable hands when given a route tree. With a 2.7% target share, though, the receiving profile is more proof of concept than production.

The path forward runs entirely through Derrick Henry's workload, and Ali has barely 17 career carries to argue he's the next man up. Going near the back of drafts, he's a deep-league handcuff lottery ticket: ignore him in standard formats and only speculate if Henry's availability wobbles.

11575.name: Ray Davis
11575.headline: Cook's Handcuff With Quiet Efficiency Under The Hood
11575.body:
Davis spent 2025 buried behind James Cook, drawing just a 14.3% snap share and 58 carries, yet the underlying work held up. He averaged 4.74 yards per attempt, posted a 0.099 EPA per play, and forced enough missed tackles to keep his explosive-run rate north of 15%. The receiving role is real too: 13 targets, 10 grabs, 1.34 yards per route run, and two scores through the air despite a microscopic 2.7% target share. The touchdown variance is the catch, as he reached the end zone only via receptions across a 17-game slate.

His 2024 ceiling, 99.1 fantasy points on 113 carries with three rushing touchdowns, shows what a workload bump unlocks. As the primary backup, Davis is a pure contingency play, going around RB60 at pick 198. He is worth a final-round dart only for Cook managers buying insurance, with standalone value tethered entirely to the starter's health.

11583.name: Jonathon Brooks
11583.headline: High-upside lottery ticket if the knee holds
11583.body:
Our profile on Brooks is built on a sliver of data: a 3-game cameo in 2024 where he logged just 9 carries for 22 yards (2.44 per rush), 3 catches on 3 targets, and an 11% snap share before his second ACL tear wiped everything out. The efficiency markers were ugly small-sample numbers — a minus-0.11 EPA per play, 1.56 yards after contact per attempt, and a single broken tackle — but the volume was far too thin to read anything into. He finished as RB111 on the back of 4.5 fantasy points.

So the bet here is talent and opportunity, not production. Brooks reportedly returned to 11-on-11 work and projects fully healthy entering 2026, slotting behind Chuba Hubbard in a thin Carolina backfield. With an ADP near pick 108, he's a cheap swing at a potential lead role. The downside is real after consecutive knee surgeries, but the ceiling justifies the late-round stab.

11589.name: Trey Benson
11589.headline: Explosive on tape, buried on the depth chart
11589.body:
Benson's 2025 was a four-game tease: 5.52 yards per carry on just 29 totes, a 52-yard burst, and a 13.79% explosive-run rate that flashed real juice. The problem was efficiency under the hood and volume on top of it. His -0.184 EPA per play and a meager 2.6% target share (0.9 yards per route run) signal a back who hasn't earned passing-down trust, and his snap share never translated into goal-line work. The 2024 baseline (4.62 YPC, one touchdown over 12 games) tells the same story: bursty, but boom-or-bust and light on scoring equity.

Now the runway has vanished. Arizona reshaped the room this offseason, pushing Benson toward the back of the rotation behind fresher additions. An ankle issue limiting his spring work only deepens the hole. The talent flashes are real, but without injuries ahead of him, he's a deep-stash dart throw, not a draftable asset in standard formats.

11592.name: Erick All
11592.headline: Promising profile stalled by a second torn ACL
11592.body:
All's 2024 tape flashed the chops that made Cincinnati's brass excited. Across eight games he caught 20 of 22 looks for a 90.9% catch rate, posted a 50% success rate, and generated 0.239 EPA per play on a 41.9% snap share. His 0.188 targets per route run and 1.35 yards per route run were efficient for a part-time role, even with a tiny 3.5% target share and shallow 3.27 air yards per target. The arrow pointed up.

That arrow snapped. A second career ACL tear lands him on PUP and likely costs the entire 2026 season, gutting any near-term value. His draft cost has collapsed to roughly TE47, around pick 291, and he sits rostered in almost no leagues. Treat him as a deep dynasty stash on talent alone; in redraft formats there is no reason to spend a pick until he proves the knee and reclaims real snaps.

11593.name: Brevyn Spann-Ford
11593.headline: Blocking Tight End With No Path To Targets
11593.body:
The advanced profile screams afterthought. Across 16 games in 2025, Spann-Ford ran a route on just 14.7% of dropbacks and saw a 2.1% target share, turning 13 looks into 9 grabs for 90 yards and one score. His 0.94 yards per route run and -0.049 EPA per play landed him 74th among tight ends, and a 24.9% snap rate confirms a blocking specialist rather than a pass-catcher.

There's no growth signal underneath. His route share actually shrank from 19.5% in 2024, and across two seasons he has never cleared 14 targets or topped 90 receiving yards. With Dallas already set ahead of him on the depth chart, his volume stays capped barring injuries. He sits outside the top 110 at the position in early rankings for good reason. Leave him on the wire in every format until usage meaningfully changes.

11600.name: Ja'Tavion Sanders
11600.headline: Sanders stuck in a committee that caps his ceiling
11600.body:
Sanders went the wrong way in 2025, slipping to TE52 after a TE34 finish the year prior. His efficiency cratered alongside the workload: yards per route run fell from 1.09 to 0.85, EPA per play sagged from 0.271 to 0.13, and his target share dipped to 7.1% across just 12 games before a December ankle break ended things. He did catch 85.3% of his looks and saw 10 red-zone targets, but with one touchdown and 190 yards, the volume simply isn't there.

The deeper problem is the timeshare with Tommy Tremble, who has matched him snap for snap and kept both from carrying weekly value. Sanders is moving well in his ankle rehab and projects for a healthy training camp, yet a TE39 ADP near pick 252 reflects the reality. He owns the better long-term profile, but until Carolina commits to him, he's a bench-stash dart throw, not a startable tight end.

11604.name: Brock Bowers
11604.headline: Bowers primed to reclaim the TE1 crown in 2026
11604.body:
Even compressed to 12 games by injury, Bowers finished as our TE10 in 2025, turning 86 targets into 64 grabs for 680 yards and seven scores while posting a stout 0.259 EPA per play and 112.2 fantasy points. Lean on the rookie blueprint instead: a TE1 finish in 2024 on 153 targets, 112 receptions, 1,194 yards and a commanding 25.8% target share that fueled 150.7 points. His 2025 share dipped to 17.4%, a function of missed time and a stalled attack rather than ability.

The roster math points back up. A thin receiver room leaves him the clear funnel for whoever wins the quarterback job, and nothing on the depth chart threatens his alpha role. Drafters are treating him as the position's top option around the second-third round turn, and that price is justified: healthy, he carries the highest weekly ceiling at a scarce position and a realistic path to overall TE1.

11620.name: Rome Odunze
11620.headline: Big-Play Profile, Stalled Catch Rate: Odunze's Bounce-Back Case
11620.body:
Odunze's 2025 sample is loaded with downfield juice: 13.91 air yards per target, a 25.3% air-yards share, and 0.243 EPA per play across 12 games. He commanded a 16.9% target share and 12 red-zone looks, turning that into six touchdowns and 102.1 fantasy points. The yardage stayed deep too, with 13 catches of 20-plus yards. The hole in the profile is a 48.9% catch rate dragged down by three interceptions on his targets rather than his hands, since he dropped just two of 46 catchable balls.

That catch-rate dip screams positive regression, especially with another year of Caleb Williams rapport ahead. The counter is volume: a crowded route tree featuring Loveland and Burden caps his ceiling, and his draft cost near the WR29 range bakes that in. Treat him as a touchdown-leveraged WR2 whose efficiency, not target count, drives the payoff.

11626.name: Xavier Legette
11626.headline: Legette's efficiency collapse buries any draft-day case
11626.body:
Year two went backward across the board. Legette posted a 13.3% target share on a 0.171 target-per-route rate, both down from his rookie marks, and his 0.97 yards per route run barely cleared replacement level. The advanced profile is ugly: minus-0.064 EPA per play, a 39.1% success rate, and a 54.7% catch rate that turned 64 looks into just 35 grabs for 363 yards. Even 12 red-zone targets yielded only three scores and 54.3 fantasy points across 15 games.

The 2024 baseline (16.2% target share, 1.26 YPRR, a 0.405 WOPR) shows the talent isn't gone, but the trend and the context both cut against him. Carolina threw for the league's third-fewest yards, and he projects no better than third in a WR2 race with Jalen Coker and rookie Chris Brazzell. Drafting at a WR104 ADP, he's a late-round dart, not a target.

11627.name: Troy Franklin
11627.headline: Waddle trade buries a quietly efficient breakout
11627.body:
Franklin's sophomore leap was real: he climbed from WR107 to WR35, posting 65 grabs, 709 yards and six scores on 104 targets across 17 games. The underlying work backed the box score, with a 17.8% target share, a healthy 0.473 WOPR, 1.55 yards per route and a positive 0.064 EPA per play after he sat at minus-0.348 as a rookie. A 45.2% success rate and 12.7 air yards per target show a genuine downfield role, not empty volume.

The problem is opportunity. Denver's Jaylen Waddle acquisition shoves Franklin behind Courtland Sutton and Waddle into a crowded No. 3 job, where he splits leftover looks with Marvin Mims and Pat Bryant. Projections crater toward 36 catches and 396 yards, making him a late-round dart that only matters if an injury opens snaps. The talent earns a stash; the depth chart caps the ceiling.

11628.name: Marvin Harrison
11628.headline: The Talent Is Loud, The Usage Went Quiet
11628.body:
Don't let a noisy 2025 fool you: when Harrison ran routes, the efficiency was elite. He posted 0.412 EPA per play and a 54.8% success rate, both jumps over his rookie marks, and that points to a player whose per-snap impact never cratered. The problem was volume. Across only 12 games he drew just 73 targets and an 11.8% target share, well off the 22.2% and 116 looks he commanded as a rookie, and his WOPR collapsed from 0.629 to 0.32.

The profile screams positive regression if the opportunity returns. He still averaged nearly 13 air yards per target and stayed a red-zone presence with 10 red-zone and five end-zone targets, finishing with four scores on a thin 41 catches. Restore a full route share and the downfield usage, and the WR45 finish becomes a floor, not a forecast. Buy the dip with conviction.

11629.name: Devontez Walker
11629.headline: Big-play flashes, but the runway just got crowded
11629.body:
Walker's 2025 line is a study in efficiency without volume: six grabs on eight looks across eight games, a 75% catch rate, 136 yards, and three touchdowns on just a 2% target share. The per-route work pops at 2.43 yards per route run and a 0.143 target-per-route rate, while a 2.242 EPA per play and 75% success rate confirm that nearly every touch turned into a win. With a 24.5-yard average depth of target, he is purely a field-stretcher, not a chain-mover.

That profile is the problem and the promise. Baltimore added a pair of rookie receivers this offseason, pushing Walker's path behind Zay Flowers and Rashod Bateman to a situational deep role he must re-earn. He carries an ADP near pick 295 and belongs only in the deepest formats. Bank the explosiveness, but the snaps and consistency simply are not there yet to chase him sooner.

11637.name: Keon Coleman
11637.headline: Make-or-break year for a stalled Bills receiver
11637.body:
Year two went sideways. Across 12 games Coleman commanded just a 12.3% target share on a 0.205 targets-per-route mark, turning 59 looks into 38 grabs for 404 yards and four scores. His 1.4 yards per route run slipped from 1.84 as a rookie, and the explosive element vanished: after notching five catches of 50-plus yards in 2024, he managed zero last season with a 17.3 air-yards-per-reception drop. The efficiency wasn't all bad (a 59.3% success rate, 0.224 EPA per play), but a 64.4% catch rate and 1.4 WOPR scream complementary role.

Now the runway shrinks further. Buffalo imported a proven veteran to occupy the same intermediate areas Coleman lived in, squeezing an already thin target tree. He's drifting toward pick 228 with WR4/WR5 projections, and nothing in the usage suggests a leap. Treat him as a late-round dart only if the depth chart clears.

11644.name: Cody Schrader
11644.headline: Practice-squad depth back chasing a Denver roster spot
11644.body:
The ledger on Schrader is thin by design. His only NFL exposure came in a single 2024 appearance, where he logged a 3 percent snap share, one carry for three yards, and a lone target he turned into a six-yard catch. That line produced 0.9 fantasy points and a No. 127 positional rank, with a modest 0.038 EPA per play across a sample far too small to read into.

He enters 2026 on a future contract with Denver after spending last season on their practice squad, which frames him as a camp body fighting for the back end of the depth chart rather than a draftable asset. The backfield ahead of him is crowded, the schedule grades out as one of the league's tougher run environments, and no ADP exists because no market does. File him as a deep stash worth tracking only if injuries thin the room.

11646.name: Jalen Coker
11646.headline: Efficiency Says Coker Earns The No. 2 Job
11646.body:
The underlying numbers love Coker even when the box score stays quiet. Across 11 games in 2025 he posted a 0.506 EPA per play and a 62.8% success rate, catching 33 of 43 targets at a 76.7% clip with zero drops. The volume cap is real, though: a 9% target share, 0.16 targets per route, and just 1.47 yards per route mean he needs more routes to matter weekly. His 2024 tape was sharper still, with 1.85 yards per route and a 0.632 EPA, signaling the talent is there once snaps climb.

Behind Tetairoa McMillan, Coker profiles as Carolina's clear second receiver, but he must fend off rookie Chris Brazzell for the 80%-plus snap rate that unlocks his value. Health has been the recurring obstacle. With a fresh extension and an ascending finish, he is a late-round flier worth the dart if Bryce Young takes a step.

11649.name: Kendall Milton
11649.headline: Milton waits behind Brown as deep insurance
11649.body:
There is almost nothing on the ledger yet. Milton logged a single game in 2025, took just two carries for two yards, and never crossed a 6% snap share. A 137th positional rank and 0.2 fantasy points tell the whole story, even if the lone bright spot, a 0.429 EPA per play across that tiny sample, hints at the burst that made the 230-pound Georgia product an intriguing developmental back.

The path to relevance runs entirely through opportunity, not pedigree. With Chase Brown entrenched as the lead back and Khalil Herbert the likely next man up, Milton sits as a third-string insurance piece carrying no meaningful draft value and a dynasty number outside the top 175. He is a watch-list stash only, worth a roster spot in the deepest formats if an injury ahead of him suddenly cracks the depth chart open.

11716.name: Tip Reiman
11716.headline: Blocking Tight End Stuck Behind McBride's Shadow
11716.body:
Reiman's profile is a blocker's profile, and the numbers leave no ambiguity. Across three games in 2025 he saw just five targets on a 0.8% target share, turning 40 routes into 18 yards and 1.8 fantasy points before an ankle issue ended the year. His 0.45 yards per route run and 0.125 targets per route run confirm he is rarely a read in the passing game, and a 0.001 EPA per play means he added essentially nothing when the ball came his way.

2024 told the same story: 41.2% snaps but a 1.3% target share and 0.24 yards per route run over eight games. The role is in-line muscle, not receiving volume. With Trey McBride entrenched and Elijah Higgins competing for the No. 2 job, there is no realistic path to fantasy relevance. Leave him on the wire in every format until the depth chart changes.

11783.name: Ryan Flournoy
11783.headline: Elite efficiency, but the targets just aren't there yet
11783.body:
Flournoy's 2025 leap was real where it counts: 0.511 EPA per play and a 57.1% success rate ranked among the most efficient marks for any wideout, a massive jump from his -0.2 EPA rookie season. He turned 56 targets into 40 catches (71.4%), 475 yards, and 4 touchdowns across 15 games, posting a healthy 1.83 yards per route run with 7 red-zone looks. The talent and the per-snap production are undeniable.

The ceiling is capped by volume, not ability. A 9.2% target share and 44.8% snap rate place him third in line behind CeeDee Lamb and George Pickens, and his biggest outings came when Lamb sat. With a WR66 draft profile, he's a dynasty stash and an injury-contingent bench piece rather than a weekly redraft option. If a spot opens ahead of him, the efficiency suggests he'd run with it.

11806.name: Casey Washington
11806.headline: Deep-field flier whose snaps still haven't arrived
11806.body:
The profile reads like a player still searching for a foothold. Across five games in 2025 Washington ran just 94 routes, a 16.5% route rate that capped his target share at 2.7% and a 0.149 targets-per-route mark that never translated into volume. He hauled in 6 of 14 looks for 94 yards and no scores, a 42.9% catch rate dragged down by a sky-high 18.71 air yards per target. The efficiency flickers were real, though: a 0.266 EPA per play and a 1.00 yards-per-route-run hint at downfield juice on a tiny sample.

The role is the problem. Stuck deep on the Atlanta depth chart and recently a healthy scratch, he carries no guaranteed targets and an ECR near pick 574. A favorable late-season schedule offers nothing if he can't crack the rotation. Treat him as a watch-list dart, not a draftable asset, until the snap share climbs out of the teens.

12062.name: Cam Grandy
12062.headline: Deep-roster Bengals tight end still chasing snaps
12062.body:
Grandy remains a developmental piece who has yet to crack Cincinnati's rotation. His 2025 sample barely registered: two games, a 9.2% snap share, just five routes run, and two targets turned into two catches for seven yards. A 1.4 yards-per-route-run mark looks tidy, but a minus-0.176 EPA per play, a single fumble, and a 0.7% route share confirm how little he was trusted on the field.

The lone encouraging note traces to 2024, when 34 routes produced a 0.556 EPA per play, a 60% success rate, and two red-zone looks including an end-zone target. That flash of efficiency is the only reason to monitor him. With negligible roster rates and a tight-end ranking buried outside the top 100, he is a watch-list name, not a draftable asset, until the depth chart opens real opportunity ahead of him.

12374.name: Brenden Bates
12374.headline: Browns' depth tight end with a buried fantasy role
12374.body:
Bates barely registered in 2025, drawing five targets across five games while logging just a 27% snap share and a 6.9% route rate. The flashes were real but tiny: he caught four of those looks (80% catch rate), posted a 0.61 EPA per play and an 80% success rate, and ran routes at 1.14 yards per route run. With a 0.9% target share, a 0.119 target-per-route-run mark, and zero touchdowns, none of it translated to usable scoring — 4.8 fantasy points all year.

The arrow points sideways. He returns to Cleveland for another season buried in a crowded tight end room, where his snaps were rotational at best and his 42 routes left no path to volume. Drafted around TE142 in early rankings with no measurable ADP, he is a name to know only in the deepest dynasty formats. Until the depth chart clears, Bates stays off redraft boards entirely.

12457.name: Jaydon Blue
12457.headline: Explosive flashes, but Blue's rookie efficiency was thin
12457.body:
Blue's rookie tape was a study in contradiction. Across five games he handled just 38 carries on a 25.6% snap share, turning them into 129 yards at a meager 3.39 per pop with one score. The efficiency underneath was alarming: a minus-0.195 EPA per play, only 1.16 yards after contact per attempt, and seven carries swallowed for negative yardage. Yet the burst is real, with a 13.16% explosive-run rate, a 27-yard long, and a breakaway gear most backs his age lack.

The path forward is opportunity, not talent. He enters camp battling for the clear backup job behind Javonte Williams, and his late-season Week 18 start showed he can shoulder a real load when handed one. At an RB65-ish ADP, he's a cheap dart with standalone upside the moment Williams misses time. Until that backup role is locked, Blue is a speculative bench stash with league-winning handcuff equity.

12469.name: Dylan Sampson
12469.headline: Sampson's receiving spark is the late-round PPR play
12469.body:
The rookie tape screams pass-catcher. Across 15 games on just a 26.5% snap rate, Sampson turned 40 targets into 33 catches and a 1.82 yards per route run mark that ranked among the best at the position, all while drawing a 7.7% target share. The ground game lagged badly: 65 carries netted 2.69 yards a pop with zero rushing scores, a minus-0.095 EPA per play, and only 1.54 yards after contact, leaving him at RB54 on 54.6 fantasy points.

At 20, the path to volume runs through the backfield's health. With Jerome Ford gone, the third-down job is open, and Sampson already worked first-team reps while Quinshon Judkins rehabs a leg injury. Going around RB52 with an RB50 consensus rank, he's a sensible late-round dart in PPR, with real handcuff upside if Judkins misses time.

12472.name: Raheim Sanders
12472.headline: Deep-League Dart Behind Cleveland's Crowded Backfield
12472.body:
Sanders' rookie tape was a small, inefficient sample: 27 carries for 92 yards (3.41 per pop) and one short touchdown across four games, with a modest 25.8% snap share. The advanced numbers hint at a runner who grinds rather than pops, churning 2.22 yards after contact per attempt against just 1.19 before it, with only one carry of 10-plus and zero breakaways. His 0.067 EPA per play and four targets on a 0.8% target share leave little for fantasy to grab right now, which is how he lands at RB88 in our rankings.

The path to relevance is purely about opportunity. Quinshon Judkins owns the early-down work, and Dylan Sampson is the other body fighting for the backup role, so Sanders enters camp as a No. 3 with a draft cost near pick 371 and roughly half a percent rostered. He's a handcuff-of-a-handcuff: only worth a deep-league flier, and only if injuries reshuffle the depth chart ahead of him.

12473.name: Mitchell Evans
12473.headline: Touchdown Lottery Ticket Waiting On A Real Role
12473.body:
Evans graded out as the TE57 last season, and the underlying numbers explain why: 14 games, a 37.4% snap rate, and just 142 routes that produced 25 targets and a 5.2% target share. The volume is thin, but the efficiency flickers. He posted a 68% success rate, 0.128 EPA per play, a 76% catch rate, and 1.2 yards per route run, turning limited looks into 19 grabs for 171 yards and two scores. Four red-zone targets, three of them inside the ten, hint at where his value lives.

That scoring upside is the entire case right now. With a 0.176 targets-per-route mark and a sub-0.10 WOPR, he is a touchdown-dependent flier rather than a weekly play. Carried at roughly TE60 in early drafts, Evans is purely a deep-league dart. If the snaps and route volume climb in Carolina's pecking order, the red-zone profile gives him a real path; until then, hold the pick.

12489.name: RJ Harvey
12489.headline: Harvey's Receiving Chops Carry Crowded Backfield Risk
12489.body:
Harvey's rookie line tells a split story: 540 rushing yards on just 3.7 per carry across 146 attempts, but a far stickier receiving profile with 47 catches on a 9.9% target share, 356 yards and five receiving scores. His efficiency as a pure runner was middling, evidenced by a slightly negative -0.014 EPA per play, 1.79 yards after contact, and 17 carries stuffed for negative yardage. The juice came in chunks, three runs of 30-plus and a 50-yard touchdown, plus seven rushing scores and 39 red-zone opportunities that pushed him to 159.6 fantasy points and an RB29 finish over 17 games.

The 2026 picture is murkier. Dobbins is back healthy and rookie Jonah Coleman crowds the goal-line work, capping the volume that fueled Harvey's late-season RB13 surge. He profiles as a passing-down asset and PPR flex rather than a featured back, with a labrum surgery worth tracking through camp. Draft him as a Round 7 upside swing.

12491.name: Corey Kiner
12491.headline: Efficient on a sliver of snaps, buried on the depth chart
12491.body:
The 2025 sample is thin but flattering: 12 carries for 58 yards at 4.83 a pop, a tidy 0.097 EPA per play, and 2.42 yards after contact per attempt across just four games on a 13.2% snap share. He even flashed receiving value with two grabs on three targets, posting a 0.5 target share when on the field. Nothing here screams future bell cow, but the per-touch numbers say he didn't waste his looks.

The problem is opportunity, not talent. Kiner sits deep in a crowded Arizona backfield, stuck behind a wall of backs and unlikely to climb without injuries ahead of him. With no rushing scores and a ranking outside the top 130 at the position, he's a roster-clogger in standard formats. File him as a deep-dynasty stash whose efficiency earns a watch if the depth chart thins.

12492.name: Pat Bryant
12492.headline: Efficient rookie flashes, but the volume isn't there
12492.body:
Bryant's rookie tape rewards a second look. Across 13 games he ran 291 routes on a 45.8% route share, drawing 49 targets for 31 grabs, 378 yards and a score. The efficiency markers pop: a 0.29 EPA per play, a 57.1% success rate, and eight red-zone looks with four trips inside the 10. He wasn't asked to do much downfield (8.4% target share, 1.3 yards per route run), yet he converted what came his way.

The problem is the opportunity ceiling, not the player. Denver added another mouth to feed, slotting Bryant behind Courtland Sutton and into a crowded battle with Troy Franklin and Marvin Mims for the No. 3 role. At a WR85 draft cost he's a late-round dart, not a roster lock. Bank the per-snap quality and the goal-line involvement as the upside; a midseason injury ahead of him is the realistic path to relevance.

12503.name: Isaiah Bond
12503.headline: Boom-or-bust field-stretcher buried on a crowded depth chart
12503.body:
Bond's rookie tape reads like a deep-shot specialist still searching for a role. Across 15 games he played just 48.1% of snaps, drew a thin 8.4% target share, and converted only 18 of 44 looks for 338 yards and no scores. The 40.9% catch rate is ugly, but it's a byproduct of usage: his 17.07 air yards per target ranked among the most aggressive in the league, and he posted a 18.78 yard average with a 0.265 WOPR. The efficiency cratered, though, with a negative EPA per play and a 31.8% success rate that landed him at WR82.

The path forward is narrow. Cleveland spent early draft capital on two rookie receivers, stacking competition in front of a player who never locked down a snap count. His 1.03 yards per route run doesn't force the issue, and there's no clear projection for volume until he earns it. Treat him as a late-round dart with big-play upside, nothing more.

12506.name: Harold Fannin
12506.headline: Fannin's rookie usage screams breakout in year two
12506.body:
As a 21-year-old rookie, Fannin already commanded a 20.5% target share across 16 games, drawing 107 looks on a 72.2% route rate and an 0.244 targets-per-route-run mark that reads like a featured pass-catcher, not a part-time tight end. He turned that volume into 72 grabs for 731 yards and six touchdowns, posting a 0.427 WOPR and just a 3.7% drop rate. The efficiency lagged the workload at 1.67 yards per route and 10.15 per catch, but a 42.1% success rate and positive EPA per play signal a player the offense trusts.

The profile is built on opportunity, and there is room to climb. Ten red-zone targets and three end-zone looks hint at touchdown upside that should expand with more snaps than last year's 75.9%. With elite YAC ability shown by 4.89 yards after the catch per reception, Fannin projects as a TE1 ascending into a larger role at age 22.

12512.name: Quinshon Judkins
12512.headline: Workhorse usage masks a rookie efficiency problem
12512.body:
Judkins handled 230 carries across 14 games as a rookie and finished the RB24, but the surface counting stats hide some warts. He averaged a thin 3.6 yards per carry, posted a negative -0.126 EPA per play, and forced a broken tackle just once every 20.9 attempts, gaining only 2.2 yards after contact. The volume was real, though: 33 red-zone opportunities and 11 goal-line carries turned into seven scores, and four runs of 30-plus yards show the home-run gear is there.

The receiving role barely exists, with a 6.9% target share, 26 grabs, and 0.96 yards per route run capping his PPR ceiling. He's recovering from a Week 16 ankle and fibula fracture but tracking toward the opener. A rebuilt line should lift the efficiency floor. Bank on a 300-touch projection and draft him as a high-volume RB2 with real touchdown equity.

12517.name: Colston Loveland
12517.headline: Loveland's Rookie Spike Points to a TE1 Leap
12517.body:
The rookie tape was loud. Across 16 games Loveland posted a 0.591 EPA per play and a 59.8% success rate on his targets, elite efficiency for a 21-year-old learning the position. He turned 82 targets into 58 grabs, 713 yards and six scores while running routes on 63% of dropbacks, and his 0.218 targets per route run plus a 15.4% target share signal a player the offense already trusts. He dropped exactly one catchable ball.

The red-zone usage is what pushes the projection higher: 14 red-zone looks and six end-zone targets at age 21 hint at a scoring floor that climbs with volume. At 1.9 yards per route run he is still rounding out as a downfield weapon, but the late-season target surge and a tightening rapport with Caleb Williams make this an obvious year-two ascent. He profiles as a top-five tight end with genuine top-three upside.

12519.name: Luther Burden
12519.headline: The efficiency is real, the volume isn't yet
12519.body:
Burden's rookie tape screams breakout the moment you account for the workload. On just 225 routes and a 37.8% route share, he posted a 0.501 EPA per play and a 58.3% success rate -- elite marks that usually belong to entrenched starters, not 21-year-olds buried at 39.7% snaps. He turned 60 targets into 47 catches (78.3% catch rate), 652 yards, and 2.9 yards per route run, flashing real after-catch juice at 7.1 YAC per reception.

The ceiling case is obvious; the floor is the problem. An 11.3% target share and 0.234 WOPR tell you the volume simply wasn't there, and Rome Odunze plus Colston Loveland complicate any leap in Ben Johnson's offense. If the slot role expands to match the per-route efficiency, Burden is a league-winner. Draft him as the high-variance upside swing he is -- pay a round early or pass.

12523.name: Jimmy Horn
12523.headline: Efficient rookie flashes await a bigger Year 2 route share
12523.body:
Horn closed his rookie year ranked 143rd among receivers, but the underlying work hinted at more than the box score showed. Across 10 games he ran routes on just 13.5% of snaps, yet still posted 0.203 targets per route run and a tidy 1.46 yards per route run. A 73.3% catch rate, 0.231 EPA per play and a 46.7% success rate all point to a runner who delivered when the ball found him, even with a microscopic 3.1% target share.

The problem was volume, not production: 15 targets and zero touchdowns leave plenty of runway. At age 22 with an 18.2% avoided-tackle rate and yards-after-catch chops, Horn profiles as a Year 2 breakout candidate the moment the snap count climbs. If the route rate doubles, his per-route efficiency suggests a real flex ceiling. For now, treat him as a cheap upside dart with the underlying metrics on his side.

12524.name: Shedeur Sanders
12524.headline: Efficiency Red Flags Cloud Sanders' Browns Audition
12524.body:
The 2025 tape is hard to spin. Across eight games Sanders posted a brutal minus-0.232 EPA per play and a 36.7% success rate, with a minus-5 CPOE confirming he completed fewer passes than expectation. He threw 10 picks against just seven scores, completed only 56.6% of his attempts, and averaged a tiny 4.8 air yards per completion while leaning on dink-and-dunk volume. The protection cratered too: a 40.3% pressure rate, 23 sacks and a 3.24-second time to throw that invited the rush.

There are slivers of upside. He flashed deep, hitting four passes of 50-plus yards and a 66-yard long, and added 169 rushing yards at 9.3 per scramble. Cleveland is still an open competition with Watson, and a new staff plus fresh line and receiver investments could lift the floor. But until the efficiency turns, he is a Superflex-only dart at QB34 ADP, not a one-QB roster piece.

12526.name: Tetairoa McMillan
12526.headline: Volume monster ready to crash the WR1 tier
12526.body:
McMillan's rookie résumé screams alpha. He commanded a 25.4% target share and a staggering 40.9% air-yards share on 122 looks, posting 70 grabs for 1,014 yards and seven scores across all 17 games. The underlying numbers back the workload: 0.236 targets per route, a 0.667 WOPR, 1.96 yards per route run, and a plus-0.207 EPA per play with a 50% success rate. That production landed him at WR14 despite an offense that gave him little margin.

The path to a true breakout is paved by sheer opportunity. Fifteen red-zone targets and an 11.6-yard average depth signal a complete, every-down role with year-two upside. The catch rate (57.4%) and eight drops leave obvious room to climb, and any quarterback stability lifts his ceiling further. An ankle issue bears monitoring, but a healthy camp keeps him squarely in the early-Round-3 conversation as a high-floor WR1 bet.

12531.name: Trevor Etienne
12531.headline: Buried in Carolina's backfield, but the burst is real
12531.body:
Etienne barely cracked the rotation as a rookie, logging a 5.6% snap share across 17 games and finishing 90th at the position with just 10.7 fantasy points. The volume was minuscule, 20 carries and three targets, yet the efficiency flashed: 4.7 yards per attempt, a 15% explosive-run rate, a 22-yard long, and 2.3 yards after contact per carry on a roster that simply never fed him. The -0.094 EPA per play and 0.72 yards per route run reflect the tiny sample more than the talent.

The problem is the depth chart, not the player. He sits behind Chuba Hubbard and Jonathon Brooks, a logjam that keeps him off redraft radars at an ADP near 341 overall. Still just 21, he profiles as a worthwhile dynasty stash. Brooks' injury history offers a believable path to real touches, and the contact balance suggests he'd hold up if that door opens.

12534.name: Kyle Monangai
12534.headline: Monangai's Goal-Line Role Makes Him a Premium Swift Handcuff
12534.body:
Monangai's rookie line was quietly efficient: 169 carries for 783 yards at 4.63 per pop, five scores, and an RB26 finish worth 128.7 fantasy points across 17 games. He cleared 4 yards per carry while operating on just 40.9 percent of snaps, with 39 red-zone opportunities and 10 goal-line totes signaling Chicago already trusts him near the stripe. The -0.029 EPA per play and modest 1.64 yards after contact temper the ceiling, but an 8.88 percent explosive-run rate shows the burst is real.

The passing-game work stays thin, with 30 targets, a 5.6 percent target share, and a 0.67 YPRR that won't fuel PPR weeks on its own. That keeps him behind D'Andre Swift as a complementary early-down piece and back-end flex. The appeal is leverage: if Swift misses time, Monangai's proven goal-line volume hands you weekly RB1/2 upside, making him a draft-and-stash worth a mid-round dart.

12543.name: Tahj Brooks
12543.headline: Buried in Cincinnati's backfield until the workload shifts
12543.body:
Brooks barely cracked the rotation as a rookie, logging just a 4.5% snap share across 12 games and turning 16 carries into 45 yards at 2.81 a pop. The advanced profile is brutal: a minus-0.672 EPA per play, 1.25 yards after contact per attempt, zero touchdowns, and a 0.3% target share that erases any passing-down floor. A line of 5.4 fantasy points and an RB112 finish tells you everything about his current leverage in the offense.

The receiving usage simply isn't there, with one catch on two looks and a 0.39 yards-per-route-run mark that won't earn him third-down snaps. A late-season concussion only muddied his standing further. Until an injury ahead of him or a depth-chart shift hands him real volume, he's a deep-stash dart throw at an RB95 valuation and a draft cost near pick 308, not a player worth chasing on draft day.

12658.name: Jackson Hawes
12658.headline: Hyper-efficient on tiny volume, Hawes needs the snaps
12658.body:
Hawes was one of the most ruthlessly efficient tight ends nobody used in 2025, posting a 0.781 EPA per play and a 73.7% success rate while finishing as the TE53. The catch: that came on just 19 targets across 13 games, a 22.7% route share, and a 4% target share. When he was thrown to, he delivered, hauling in 16 of 18 catchable balls at an 84.2% clip for 187 yards, 11.7 per grab, and three scores. The 1.55 yards per route run is real; the opportunity is not.

The profile is a blocking-first TE2 buried behind Dalton Kincaid, and the path to relevance runs entirely through a Kincaid absence. Until the snap count climbs past 44%, the receiving production stays a rounding error. He is an end-of-draft dart and a deep-league injury stash, not a weekly asset, but the efficiency floor means any volume bump would play up fast.

12699.name: LaJohntay Wester
12699.headline: Return specialist with a long shot at offensive snaps
12699.body:
Wester arrives in Baltimore as a return man first and a receiver second. The front office has openly floated him as a potential impact returner, and his college resume backs the projection: 66 punt returns spread across five seasons at FAU and Colorado give him real reps in the role the Ravens are penciling him into for 2026. At 5-foot-9 and 170 pounds, he profiles as a twitchy slot type whose fastest path to value runs through special teams rather than the wide-receiver rotation.

For fantasy purposes, the arrow points down for now. He carries a roughly 360 overall ADP, sits around WR151, and is essentially unrostered across the market. Until he carves out offensive snaps behind an established Baltimore pass-catching group, treat him as a deep-league dart and waiver stash whose ceiling depends entirely on injuries opening targets ahead of him.

12738.name: Phil Mafah
12738.headline: Mafah's lone cameo flashed efficiency, but the runway is short
12738.body:
Mafah's rookie ledger is a single Week 18 snapshot, yet the underlying marks intrigue. On just five carries he averaged 3.6 yards a pop with a goal-line touchdown, chipped in two grabs for an absurd 3.67 yards per route run, and posted a 0.133 EPA per play across a tiny 18 percent snap share. The 8.9 fantasy points in one game read as noise, but a back gaining 2.2 yards before contact on a thin sample at least hints the burst is real.

The problem is the depth chart, not the talent. Mafah enters camp battling for the backup job behind Javonte Williams, and at an RB ADP buried near pick 316 the market sees a year-two flier, not a workload. He carries late-round dart appeal only if he wins the No. 2 role outright; until that clarity arrives, he is a watch-list stash rather than a draftable name.

12797.name: Ahmani Marshall
12797.headline: Reserve/Future Flier Stuck Deep On Cleveland's Bench
12797.body:
Marshall enters 2026 on the very edge of the roster picture, having inked a reserve/future deal with Cleveland after spending time on the practice squad. The Appalachian State product brings genuine size for the position at 6-foot-1 and 225 pounds, and at 24 he is young enough to develop, but nothing about his current standing suggests a defined role is waiting. He sits behind the established Browns backfield with no clear path to touches as offseason work begins.

The deep-league ranking near RB210 and essentially zero roster rate tell the story: this is a watch-list name, not a draftable asset. Cleveland's schedule grades out roughly middle-of-the-pack for backs, so opportunity, not matchup, is the only thing holding him back. Monitor camp and preseason for any sign he climbs the depth chart, but until that happens he belongs nowhere near redraft boards.

12851.name: Rivaldo Fairweather
12851.headline: Camp body buried at the bottom of Arizona's depth chart
12851.body:
Fairweather lands as pure roster filler in Arizona after the Cardinals plucked him off the Cowboys practice squad late last year. At 23, the 6-3, 249-pound Auburn product profiles as a developmental depth piece rather than a target earner, and the expert board reflects that reality: he sits at TE170 in consensus rankings, essentially undraftable in standard formats and rostered in almost no leagues.

The projection math is brutal for fantasy purposes, with the field forecasting roughly a dozen catches, a little over 100 yards, and a single score across the full season. A 28th-ranked strength of schedule only deepens the hole. Until injuries clear a path or he wins a real role in the tight end room, there is no startable case here. Leave him on the wire and revisit only if the depth chart collapses in front of him.

12897.name: Nathan Carter
12897.headline: Flashes on a tiny sample, buried on Atlanta's depth chart
12897.body:
Carter's 2025 rookie ledger is microscopic: two games, nine carries, 60 yards. The efficiency, though, popped. He averaged 6.67 yards a tug, ripped off two runs of 10-plus for a 22.2 percent explosive-run rate, and reached 12 yards on his longest scamper. Those are encouraging flashes from a 23-year-old who barely touched the field.

Temper the optimism, because the rest of the profile screams depth piece. His -0.181 EPA per play, a fumble on nine carries, and a No. 107 positional finish with just four fantasy points all reflect a player still fighting for snaps. Zero red-zone or goal-line work means no touchdown equity, and zero targets cap any passing-game upside. Until injuries or attrition clear a path in Atlanta's backfield, Carter stays off draftable radars as a deep-stash dart throw rather than a roster lock.

13079.name: Jahdae Walker
13079.headline: Tiny Sample, Loud Efficiency: A Caleb Williams Lottery Ticket
13079.body:
Walker's 2025 was a six-game cameo, but the per-route numbers pop. On just 57 routes and a 9.6% route rate, he posted 1.53 yards per route, a 0.175 target-per-route-run mark, and a sky-high 0.685 EPA per play with a 60% success rate. The volume was minimal -- a 1.9% target share and 0.047 WOPR -- yet he turned 10 looks into 6 grabs, 87 yards, and two scores, including two end-zone targets. That 20.7-point flash on 15.1% of snaps shows real juice when the ball finds him.

The ceiling depends entirely on snaps. At 6-foot-3 and 23, he profiles as a downfield, red-zone weapon for Caleb Williams, but he's buried in the rotation and undraftable at a 300-overall ADP. Treat him as a deep-league stash: the efficiency is intriguing, but until the route share climbs meaningfully, the production stays a rounding error rather than a roster lock.

13268.name: Elijah Sarratt
13268.headline: Deep-league sleeper buried on a crowded Ravens depth chart
13268.body:
A fourth-round rookie out of Indiana, Sarratt lands in a Baltimore receiver room with little room to breathe. Zay Flowers owns the top job, and fellow rookie Ja'Kobi Lane is slotted for outside snaps, pushing the 6-foot-2, 210-pound Sarratt inside despite a college profile built on perimeter work. Expert consensus parks him around WR96 with an ADP near 204, and projections of roughly 20 grabs, 295 yards, and under two scores tell you how thin the Year 1 path looks.

The appeal is purely upside. Sarratt wins contested balls and earns targets at a level that hints at relevance if an injury opens snaps ahead of him. Realistically, the target volume in this offense isn't there yet, so treat him as a dynasty stash or last-pick dart in very deep redraft formats rather than a roster lock.

13287.name: Jeremiyah Love
13287.headline: Love's draft capital screams workhorse — if Arizona lets him cook
13287.body:
Arizona spent the third overall pick on Love, and a team doesn't burn that kind of capital to hand a back a timeshare. The Notre Dame product arrives with a monster resume — roughly 2,497 rushing yards and 35 scores on 362 carries across his final two seasons, plus 55 grabs — and steps into a featured role at just 21. Drafters are buying in, pushing his ADP up around 23rd overall and inside the top 13 at the position.

The only thing between Love and a top-12 finish is the depth chart. James Conner and Tyler Allgeier loom as touchdown vultures, and a shaky quarterback room caps the scoring environment. But the talent gap is real, and the logic is obvious: this backfield consolidates around Love sooner than later. Treat him as a high-end RB2 with legitimate top-10 upside once the workload tilts his way.

13293.name: Ja'Kobi Lane
13293.headline: Ravens rookie is a red-zone bet for patient dynasty rosters
13293.body:
A third-round pick out of USC, Ja'Kobi Lane lands in Baltimore as a 6-4, 200-pound vertical threat whose combine measurables jump off the page, headlined by 10.5-inch hands and one of the longest wingspans in the class. The athletic profile fits exactly what Baltimore wants near the goal line, and the coaching staff has openly signaled it intends to weaponize his size in scoring situations. The route nuance and ball-tracking are real, even if an 8.6 percent college drop rate and thin yards-after-catch chops flag a developmental runway.

The path to volume is the sticking point. Rashod Bateman is locked in through 2029, and Lane must climb past Elijah Sarratt for steady snaps, which keeps his Year 1 ceiling capped. Drafting near pick 233 overall, he is a stash, not a starter. Treat him as a dynasty target in the late-second to early-third tier and let the redraft case build itself before committing a bench spot.

13297.name: Reggie Virgil
13297.headline: Big-bodied rookie buried on a crowded depth chart
13297.body:
A fifth-round pick (143rd overall) out of college, Virgil arrives in Arizona with a prototypical 6'3", 187-pound frame but lands behind an established trio. Marvin Harrison Jr. and Michael Wilson lock down the top two roles, leaving the rookie to scrap with Kendrick Bourne for snaps on the back end. His final college campaign produced 57 grabs for 705 yards and six scores, useful tape but not the kind of profile that forces early targets.

The scouting read is a possession-style receiver: ordinary speed, modest yards after the catch, and a route tree that still needs work, though he reads zone coverage well. Projections peg him near 3-4 catches and roughly 39 yards a game, which translates to fringe relevance at best. Leave him on the waiver wire in redraft and treat him as a late-round dart only in deep rookie dynasty formats.

13298.name: KC Concepcion
13298.headline: Late-Round Browns Lottery Ticket With Real Starter Upside
13298.body:
A first-round selection out of Texas A&M, Concepcion arrives in Cleveland with the kind of explosiveness that turns short catches into chunk plays, separating after the grab and making defenders miss in space. His final college campaign produced 919 receiving yards and nine scores, a total that likely undersold him given erratic deep-ball accuracy from his quarterbacks. The fit isn't perfect: Jerry Jeudy and fellow rookie Denzel Boston will siphon looks, and the drop problems that nagged him in college could carry over.

The ceiling is what makes him worth the gamble. Among rookie receivers available well past pick 100, he profiles as the most likely to seize a starting-caliber workload, with a path to becoming Cleveland's top passing-game target. The comparison points, Ladd McConkey and Brian Thomas Jr., both blossomed into top-24 finishers. A shaky Browns aerial attack caps the floor, but the cost is minimal and the payoff could be a weekly contributor.

13302.name: Adam Randall
13302.headline: Raw Ravens convert buried behind Henry's workload
13302.body:
Randall arrives in Baltimore as a fifth-round developmental swing, a 6-foot-3, 232-pound former Clemson receiver with a single season of college running back reps under his belt. That thin resume shows up in the draft data: an overall ADP near 259 and an expert consensus rank closer to 294, which is a polite way of saying he is essentially undrafted in standard redraft formats. He profiles as a project, not a contributor, sitting third or fourth in a Derrick Henry backfield that already houses Justice Hill and Rasheen Ali.

Projections of roughly 55 carries, 280 rushing yards and a handful of catches confirm there is no standalone path to relevance while Henry stays upright. The realistic case is a deep-dynasty stash: monitor training-camp depth-chart chatter, and let someone else absorb the upside-free volume until his receiver-to-back transition shows tangible burst. For 2026, leave him on the wire.

13312.name: Josh Kattus
13312.headline: Kattus a developmental tight end buried in Cincinnati
13312.body:
Kattus enters 2026 as a raw, late-roster project out of Kentucky, and the market reflects it: he checks in around TE22 with an expert consensus ranking near TE156 overall, no available projections, and essentially zero presence in fantasy lineups. At 6-foot-4 and 245 pounds he carries the frame teams covet at the position, but there is no meaningful NFL production to point to yet, and the Bengals' depth chart gives him a steep climb for snaps.

For managers, this is a name to track rather than draft. The schedule offers some early softness, with one of the league's friendlier opening matchups and an easy three-game stretch through Houston, Pittsburgh and Jacksonville, but opportunity has to come first. Until an injury or role change opens the door, Kattus stays a deep-league dynasty stash whose value hinges entirely on carving out a real spot in the rotation.

13320.name: Zachariah Branch
13320.headline: Branch Brings Burst, But Targets Will Be Scarce Early
13320.body:
Branch arrives in Atlanta with rare juice and a runway to the No. 2 receiver job opposite Drake London, but the math is the catch. With Bijan Robinson and Kyle Pitts ahead of him in the pecking order, he could open the year as the fourth option in the passing game, and the early projection of roughly 21 grabs for 252 yards and a touchdown reflects that crowded reality.

The draft market agrees, parking him near pick 180 and around WR91, an end-of-draft dart in redraft. His value is real in dynasty, where the elite open-field burst and college-leading screen volume hint at a manufactured-touch role that scales. For 2026, treat him as a stash and waiver target rather than a starter, betting on a second-half snap bump if the Falcons lean into his YAC ability.

13322.name: Sam Roush
13322.headline: Roush stuck third on Chicago's tight end ladder
13322.body:
A third-round pick out of Stanford, Roush lands in Chicago with the build of a classic in-line Y (6-5, 267) and a combine workout that flashed real size-and-speed upside. The problem is the room ahead of him: Colston Loveland and Cole Kmet own the meaningful snaps, leaving Roush as a developmental piece whose early reps come mostly in heavy 12 and 13 personnel under Ben Johnson. His college profile leans blocker, with limited separation speed and modest yards after the catch.

The draft market reflects all of it. He's drifting near pick 343 overall and barely cracks the top 55 at the position, essentially undraftable in standard formats. A hard early-season schedule does him no favors. Treat 2026 as a redshirt year and a deep-dynasty stash at best, not a contributor you actively roster in seasonal leagues.

13324.name: Matt Hibner
13324.headline: Hibner Lands in Baltimore as an Andrews Heir
13324.body:
Baltimore thought enough of Hibner to trade up in the fourth round for him, sending the 49ers pick 154 plus a 2027 sixth to grab the SMU product. He arrives as a fifth-year senior who flashed late, posting 31 grabs for 436 yards and four scores across 13 games in 2025 after a quiet Michigan stint. The athletic profile and formation versatility give the Ravens a chess piece they can deploy all over.

The redraft case is thin. Mark Andrews still anchors this room, and Hibner projects as a backup in a multi-tight-end scheme, so meaningful snaps likely hinge on injury. That makes him a name to stash rather than start. The real value lives in dynasty, where he profiles as the eventual successor to Andrews and a sneaky streamer if the veteran misses time. Park him on a taxi squad and let the runway develop.

13345.name: Jonah Coleman
13345.headline: Buried Behind Two Backs, But Worth A Late Dart
13345.body:
Coleman enters his rookie season stuck third on Denver's depth chart, behind J.K. Dobbins and R.J. Harvey in a crowded backfield. As a fourth-round pick at a compact 5-foot-8, 220 pounds, he profiles as a short-yardage and goal-line bruiser more than a feature option, and his draft cost reflects that with an ADP near pick 150. At Washington he piled up 1,811 yards and 25 scores on 349 carries while adding 54 grabs, flashing the contact balance that makes him intriguing if the picture clears.

The path to relevance runs through Dobbins, whose chronic foot trouble limited him to 10 games a year ago. Should that repeat, or Harvey settle into a passing-down role, Coleman's short-yardage value spikes into flex territory. He's a cheap stash with a clear injury-leverage profile, though a featured workload likely waits until 2027.

13346.name: Denzel Boston
13346.headline: Big-bodied rookie with a crowded path to targets
13346.body:
Boston arrives in Cleveland as a Day 2 pick (39th overall) after leading Washington in receiving yards, bringing a 6-foot-4, 215-pound frame, reliable hands and contested-catch chops. The trade-off is athletic: he skipped the 40 at the combine and won't separate with speed, so his game leans on size and ball skills rather than burst. His current draft cost reflects the wait-and-see profile, landing around WR63 and 158th overall.

The early-season picture is the real obstacle. He's stacked behind Jerry Jeudy and competing with KC Concepcion and Harold Fannin Jr. for a target share that won't be his by default, and Cleveland's shaky quarterback situation caps the ceiling for every pass-catcher. Treat him as a late-round dart in deeper formats and let the snap count earn your trust before he reaches your roster.

13353.name: Chris Brazzell
13353.headline: Late-Round Field Stretcher Buried On Carolina's Depth Chart
13353.body:
Carolina spent a third-round pick on the 6-foot-4 vertical threat, and the college tape backs the investment: Brazzell led the SEC with nine receiving touchdowns in his final Tennessee season while soaking up 26.7% of the offense's receiving yards. That blend of size, downfield speed and red-zone production is exactly the archetype that can stay fantasy-relevant on thin volume, even as a rookie still rounding out his route tree.

The problem for 2026 is opportunity. He opens behind Tetairoa McMillan and Jalen Coker in a passing game projected to rank among the league's worst, capping his early ceiling to splash plays rather than steady targets. Treat him as a late-round dart throw in redraft, monitoring camp for a path to the WR3 role. Dynasty managers should value him higher, banking on the profile to cash in once the offense and his usage catch up.

13355.name: DJ Rogers
13355.headline: Undrafted TCU tight end fighting for Dallas snaps
13355.body:
Rogers arrives in Dallas as an undrafted rookie out of TCU, where he hauled in 34 catches for 319 yards and a pair of scores in 2025. He profiles as a move tight end whose value lives near the line of scrimmage, with roughly 80 percent of his college targets coming within nine yards. The footwork is polished enough to win on short routes, but the speed, burst, and blocking strength all grade as ordinary, capping the ceiling.

The depth chart is the real obstacle. Jake Ferguson and Luke Schoonmaker both return ahead of him, leaving Rogers to scrap for reserve reps and special-teams value rather than a defined receiving role. That reality shows up in the ranks: he sits down near TE139 and is essentially unrostered. Treat him as a deep-stash dynasty name who only matters in standard formats if an injury opens the door.

13402.name: Skyler Bell
13402.headline: A twitchy UConn flier buried in Buffalo's committee
13402.body:
Bell arrives as a fourth-round rookie out of UConn, and the appeal is mostly about traits: a 192-pound frame, sudden acceleration, and standout work after the catch, where he forced 26 missed tackles across his final two college seasons and averaged better than eight yards after every reception. The downside is just as clear. His release work and route polish are unfinished, he struggled with drops and body catches in college, and roughly four of every five targets came on shorter routes, so the deep-field upside isn't there yet.

The bigger problem is opportunity. Buffalo runs a true receiver-by-committee, and Bell has to climb past Joshua Palmer, Keon Coleman, and Khalil Shakir just to sniff regular volume behind DJ Moore. That caps him as a speculative late-round dart in deep redraft formats and a mid-round rookie swing in dynasty, where the athletic ceiling tied to Josh Allen makes him worth a cheap bet.

13411.name: Zavion Thomas
13411.headline: Burner Rookie Buried On A Crowded Depth Chart
13411.body:
The Bears spent the 89th pick on Thomas, betting on track-meet speed rather than college production, and the bet is a gamble. His LSU profile is thin: a target share that sat in the 19th percentile and just 1.52 yards per route run, the kind of efficiency that makes the early selection a head-scratcher. At 5-foot-10 and 22 years old, he lands behind a reshuffled but still busy pass-catching room, and barring injuries ahead of him he has a steep climb toward weekly relevance.

The one real path is scheme. Ben Johnson reportedly wants a gadget role that mixes in backfield snaps, the same blueprint Green Bay used with Savion Williams. That could manufacture touches his route metrics never would. Still, Year 1 looks limited, and he is rostered in roughly 1% of leagues for good reason. Leave him on the wire and revisit only if his snap count climbs midseason.

13435.name: Joe Royer
13435.headline: Royer is a developmental stash, not a redraft target
13435.body:
Cleveland grabbed Royer 170th overall in the fifth round, and his rookie role reflects that pedigree. He enters camp battling Jack Stoll and Blake Whiteheart for backup snaps behind starter Harold Fannin Jr., so the runway to volume is narrow. A hamstring issue sidelined him through combine and pro-day testing, costing him valuable early reps in a tight end room where the depth chart is already crowded above him.

The profile is intriguing for the patient. At Cincinnati he hauled in 79 catches working primarily from the slot, ranked fourth in yards after the catch per reception in 2025, and dropped just two passes across his final two seasons. Route diversity and pass protection still need polish. With projections near four points per game and essentially zero roster ownership, treat him as a dynasty taxi-squad flier and leave him alone in redraft.

13436.name: Kentrel Bullock
13436.headline: Camp longshot eyeing a Bengals backfield foothold
13436.body:
Bullock enters 2026 as a deep roster flier in Cincinnati, currently held in essentially none of redraft leagues. At 24 and out of South Alabama, the 5-foot-10, 204-pound back has yet to carve out a defined role, sitting low on a depth chart that gives him no immediate path to touches. With no established ADP and no draft-day buzz, he profiles strictly as a watch-list name until camp clarifies the pecking order behind the starters.

The situation does him no favors either: Cincinnati's backfield draws the league's 28th-toughest schedule for running backs in 2026, squeezing whatever early-down or change-of-pace work might trickle down. Treat Bullock as a stash only in the deepest formats. He needs an injury ahead of him or a standout preseason to register on the fantasy radar, and until then there is no reason to spend a pick on the profile.

13477.name: Colbie Young
13477.headline: Stash The Bengals Rookie, But Don't Draft Him Yet
13477.body:
Cincinnati spent a fourth-round pick (140th overall) on Young, a 6-foot-5, 218-pound rookie out of Georgia who lands in one of the league's most top-heavy receiver rooms. Ja'Marr Chase and Tee Higgins lock down the targets, with Andrei Iosivas, entering a contract year, holding the next snaps. That leaves Young buried, and an ADP near 326 overall reflects it. The projection line is tiny: roughly 3.5 catches, 43 yards and a fraction of a touchdown.

The path to relevance is real but narrow. The Bengals are thin behind their two stars, so the No. 3 job is genuinely up for grabs. Young's size gives him a red-zone and contested-catch profile worth monitoring if he flashes early. For redraft purposes there's no reason to spend a pick here. In dynasty rookie formats, though, he's a cheap dart throw whose value spikes the moment an opportunity opens.

13712.name: Hayden Large
13712.headline: Bears TE is a watch-list dart, not a draft pick
13712.body:
Large enters 2026 as a developmental piece in Chicago's tight end room, a 24-year-old Iowa product with the prototypical 6-foot-4, 250-pound build teams covet at the position. The problem is opportunity: he sits near zero percent rostered, carries no established draft consensus, and hasn't earned an expert ranking, which tells you everything about where the staff sees him on the depth chart heading into the season.

The schedule does him no favors either, with the position drawing one of the league's tougher slates against opposing defenses. Treat Large as a pure speculative stash rather than a startable asset. If an injury opens a path to snaps, his size and frame make him worth a quick look, but until the targets materialize he belongs on the waiver wire in all standard formats. Monitor camp reports before spending anything.

13725.name: Dominic Richardson
13725.headline: Deep Cowboys flier with a runway worth tracking
13725.body:
Richardson lands at the far end of fantasy radars heading into 2026, carrying an expert consensus rank near RB202 and a roster rate hovering around zero percent. That single-evaluator footprint tells you everything about where the Tulsa product sits today: a developmental back on the Dallas roster fighting for snaps, not a draftable name in standard formats. At 6-foot, 204 pounds, he profiles as a between-the-tackles option who first has to earn a defined role before any usage projections make sense.

The one tailwind is context. Dallas draws one of the league's softer running-back schedules in 2026, which inflates the ceiling for whichever backs ultimately command touches. Richardson is purely a waiver-wire monitor, not a draft-day target, but injuries ahead of him on the depth chart could open a path. Stash him on your watchlist and let camp clarify whether the opportunity becomes real.

13750.name: TJ Harden
13750.headline: A deep Browns sleeper waiting on opportunity
13750.body:
Harden enters 2026 as a true under-the-radar name, rostered in essentially zero leagues and buried in expert consensus at RB171 (overall ECR around 543). At just 22 and built at 6-0, 218 out of SMU, the Cleveland back has the frame to handle real volume, but the projections and game logs simply aren't there yet, leaving him a pure depth-chart bet rather than a draftable asset on day one.

The schedule, at least, would reward a breakout if the touches ever arrive. Cleveland's slate grades out as the eighth-easiest the rest of the way, and the fantasy playoff stretch against New York, Baltimore, and Indianapolis ranks among the friendliest in the league. Treat Harden as a watch-list stash in deeper formats: monitor camp reps and injuries ahead of him, and pounce only once he carves out a defined role.

13844.name: Coleman Bennett
13844.headline: Deep Bears backfield flier with no clear path yet
13844.body:
Bennett enters 2026 as a fringe name buried on Chicago's depth chart, and the market reflects it: he carries an expert consensus rank near RB213, sits in roughly zero percent of rosters, and has drawn a grade from just a single ranker. The Kennesaw State product checks in at 5-foot-11, 185 pounds, profiling as a smaller back who has yet to carve out meaningful snaps behind the Bears' established options.

The schedule offers little reason to chase him. Chicago's backfield faces the 23rd-toughest slate of run defenses, with a tougher-than-average rest-of-season draw and a middling playoff stretch against Buffalo, Green Bay, and Detroit. Until Bennett climbs the rotation and earns real volume, he stays strictly a watch-list stash rather than a draftable asset. Monitor camp reps and any injury news ahead of him, but there's no actionable role to bank on right now.

13909.name: Elijah Tau-Tolliver
13909.headline: Deep Ravens backfield flier with size to monitor
13909.body:
Tau-Tolliver lands in Baltimore as a back-end name in a crowded backfield, currently rostered in essentially no leagues and slotted around RB214 in expert consensus. There's no draft capital priced in yet, no projection, and no clear path to early touches, so any 2026 redraft value is purely speculative until the depth chart sorts itself out behind the established options.

What keeps him on the radar is the frame. At 6'0" and 204 pounds out of Michigan State, he has the build of a between-the-tackles grinder who could carve out short-yardage or goal-line work if the Ravens thin out ahead of him. Treat him as a watch-list stash, not a draft-day target. If an injury opens a lane in this run-heavy offense, he becomes a waiver name worth a flier rather than a player you reach for now.

14044.name: Miles Davis
14044.headline: Undrafted Long Shot Fighting For Panthers Backfield Snaps
14044.body:
Davis arrives in Carolina as an undrafted free agent, and the early reality is sobering: he heads into OTAs scrapping for a depth role behind an established rotation rather than walking into carries. Expert consensus pegs him around the 150th running back and roughly 492nd overall, the kind of placement reserved for camp bodies who need an injury or a standout summer to crack the radar. He is currently on essentially zero percent of rosters.

The path forward is narrow but not closed. Carolina's backfield offers reps to whoever earns them in pads, and a strong preseason could vault him onto the practical-squad bubble. Just temper the timeline: the Panthers face the league's toughest projected schedule for backs in 2026, so even an opportunity would come against unforgiving fronts. For now, this is a watch-list dart, not a draftable asset.
